result:
**WEXFORD HALE LLP** *Attorneys at Law* 300 West 6th Street, Suite 1500 Austin, Texas 78701 Telephone: (512) 555-0177 Facsimile: (512) 555-0178

June 15, 2023

**VIA CERTIFIED MAIL --- RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED** **AND VIA EMAIL (p.espinoza@meridiancloud.com)**

Patricia M. Espinoza, Esq. General Counsel Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. 9200 Research Boulevard, Suite 400 Austin, Texas 78759

> **Re: Pre-Suit Demand Pursuant to Texas Business and Commerce Code § 17.505(a) --- Arcadia Health Systems, LLC v. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. --- Demand for \$47,300,000**

Dear Ms. Espinoza:

This firm represents Arcadia Health Systems, LLC (\"Arcadia\"), in connection with claims arising from the Master Software License and Services Agreement (\"MSLSA\") dated March 15, 2022, between Arcadia and Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. (\"Meridian\"), and the related Statement of Work #1 (\"SOW-1\") executed concurrently therewith, for the licensing and implementation of Meridian\'s NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module.

This letter constitutes a formal pre-suit demand under the Texas Deceptive Trade Practices --- Consumer Protection Act (\"DTPA\"), Texas Business and Commerce Code §§ 17.41--17.63, and specifically serves as the written notice required by Section 17.505(a) of the DTPA. As set forth below, Meridian engaged in false, misleading, and deceptive acts and practices in connection with the sale and provision of NexusCore software and related services to Arcadia, a consumer under the DTPA. Arcadia hereby demands that Meridian tender the sum of **Forty-Seven Million Three Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$47,300,000.00)** in full satisfaction of its claims within sixty (60) days of Meridian\'s receipt of this notice.

**[I. ARCADIA\'S STATUS AS A \"CONSUMER\" UNDER THE DTPA]{.underline}**

Section 17.45(4) of the DTPA defines a \"consumer\" as \"an individual, partnership, corporation, this state, or a subdivision or agency of this state who seeks or acquires by purchase or lease, any goods or services.\" Arcadia acquired goods and services from Meridian --- namely, a software license and associated implementation, training, data migration consulting, and maintenance services --- in exchange for total consideration of \$14,700,000 under the MSLSA, plus an additional \$2,350,000 pursuant to Change Orders CO-001 through CO-004. Arcadia is therefore a \"consumer\" within the meaning of the DTPA, and the protections of the Act apply fully to the transaction at issue.

**[II. FACTUAL BACKGROUND]{.underline}**

On March 15, 2022, Arcadia entered into the MSLSA with Meridian for the deployment of NexusCore, Meridian\'s cloud-based ERP platform, specifically the Healthcare Analytics Module. The total contract value was \$14,700,000, consisting of software license fees (\$6,200,000), implementation services (\$3,800,000), annual maintenance and support (\$1,900,000), training services (\$1,200,000), and data migration consulting (\$1,600,000).

Arcadia\'s decision to acquire NexusCore was the direct result of an extensive pre-sale process during which Meridian, through its Account Executive James Poletti and other representatives, made numerous material representations regarding the capabilities, performance, and compatibility of the NexusCore platform. Specifically:

> **(a)** On or about October 12, 2021, during an initial sales presentation to Arcadia\'s leadership team, Meridian represented that \"NexusCore delivers industry-leading performance for healthcare analytics\" and that Meridian\'s \"clients typically see 30-40% improvement in reporting efficiency.\"
>
> **(b)** On or about November 18, 2021, during a follow-up demonstration, Mr. Poletti affirmatively represented to Arcadia\'s personnel that NexusCore \"will integrate seamlessly with any EHR platform.\" This representation was made with knowledge of Arcadia\'s critical need for integration with its existing electronic health record systems servicing 23 hospitals and over 140 clinics across Texas and Louisiana.
>
> **(c)** Meridian represented, both orally and through its written proposal delivered December 9, 2021, that NexusCore would be implemented and operational by the target Go-Live date of September 1, 2022, and that the platform would perform reliably for Arcadia\'s healthcare analytics requirements upon deployment.

In reliance on these representations, Arcadia executed the MSLSA and SOW-1, committed \$14,700,000 in contract consideration, and undertook the substantial organizational effort of transitioning from its legacy analytics platform, HealthBridge 4.0, to NexusCore.

The NexusCore system failed to perform as represented. The Go-Live date was missed by 137 days, with actual Go-Live not achieved until January 15, 2023. Following Go-Live, the system exhibited pervasive defects, including intermittent latency issues, report generation errors, data integrity failures, and an inability to integrate reliably with Arcadia\'s EHR systems. As of the date of this letter, Arcadia continues to experience significant operational disruptions caused by the defective NexusCore platform, necessitating continued parallel operation of the legacy HealthBridge 4.0 system at enormous cost. The four additional Change Orders executed during the implementation period, totaling \$2,350,000, represent costs imposed upon Arcadia solely as a result of Meridian\'s failure to deliver a functional product within the originally contracted scope and timeline.

**[III. DTPA VIOLATIONS]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s conduct constitutes violations of the DTPA, including but not limited to the following:

**A. False, Misleading, or Deceptive Acts or Practices (§ 17.46(b))**

Meridian violated Section 17.46(b) of the DTPA by engaging in the following acts and practices in connection with the sale of NexusCore to Arcadia:

> **(1)** *Representing that goods or services have characteristics, uses, benefits, or qualities which they do not have* (§ 17.46(b)(5)). Meridian represented that NexusCore would deliver \"industry-leading performance for healthcare analytics\" and \"30-40% improvement in reporting efficiency.\" These representations were false. The NexusCore platform, as delivered, was materially deficient and incapable of performing its core analytics functions reliably.
>
> **(2)** *Representing that goods or services are of a particular standard, quality, or grade when they are of another* (§ 17.46(b)(7)). Meridian held NexusCore out as an enterprise-grade, production-ready analytics platform suitable for deployment across a multi-facility healthcare organization. The software as delivered was not of production quality and contained pervasive defects rendering it unfit for its intended purpose.
>
> **(3)** *Representing that an agreement confers or involves rights, remedies, or obligations which it does not have or involve* (§ 17.46(b)(12)). Meridian represented through Mr. Poletti that NexusCore \"will integrate seamlessly with any EHR platform,\" thereby representing that the MSLSA would confer upon Arcadia functional integration capabilities that NexusCore did not, in fact, possess.
>
> **(4)** *Failing to disclose information concerning goods or services which was known at the time of the transaction if such failure to disclose was intended to induce the consumer into a transaction the consumer would not have entered into had the information been disclosed* (§ 17.46(b)(24)). Meridian knew or should have known of the limitations of NexusCore\'s integration capabilities with certain EHR platforms and failed to disclose those limitations to Arcadia prior to execution of the MSLSA.

**B. Unconscionable Action or Course of Action (§ 17.50(a)(3))**

Meridian\'s conduct constitutes an unconscionable action or course of action within the meaning of Section 17.50(a)(3) in that Meridian took advantage of Arcadia\'s lack of knowledge regarding the true capabilities and limitations of NexusCore to a grossly unfair degree, resulting in a gross disparity between the value received by Arcadia and the consideration paid.

**C. Breach of Warranty (§ 17.50(a)(2))**

Meridian\'s delivery of a defective and nonconforming software platform constitutes a breach of express and implied warranties, which is independently actionable under DTPA Section 17.50(a)(2).

**[IV. DAMAGES]{.underline}**

As a direct and proximate result of Meridian\'s deceptive acts, practices, and misrepresentations, Arcadia has suffered damages in the aggregate amount of **\$47,300,000**, itemized as follows:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Category**                                                            **Amount**
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Direct Damages / Contract Restitution                                   \$14,700,000

  Lost Profits (5-year projection, per Grayson Whitmore & Co. analysis)   \$18,400,000

  Increased Operating Costs (parallel systems, staffing, consulting)      \$6,700,000

  Reputational Harm (loss of prospective hospital clients)                \$5,000,000

  Additional Damages Under DTPA (treble damages per § 17.50(b)(1))        \$2,500,000

  **Total**                                                               **\$47,300,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Arcadia reserves its right to seek actual damages up to three times the amount established at trial under Section 17.50(b)(1) of the DTPA should it be established that Meridian\'s conduct was committed knowingly. Arcadia further reserves its right to seek reasonable and necessary attorneys\' fees under Section 17.50(d) of the DTPA, in addition to the damages set forth above.

**[V. DEMAND]{.underline}**

Pursuant to Section 17.505(a) of the DTPA, Arcadia hereby demands that Meridian, within sixty (60) days of receipt of this notice:

> **(1)** Tender to Arcadia the sum of **\$47,300,000.00** in full and final satisfaction of all claims arising from the MSLSA, SOW-1, Change Orders CO-001 through CO-004, and all related pre-sale representations and conduct; or
>
> **(2)** Submit a reasonable settlement offer in writing, setting forth in detail the amount offered and the basis therefor.

Failure to respond to this demand, or to tender a reasonable offer of settlement, within the statutory sixty-day period will result in Arcadia filing suit in the appropriate court of competent jurisdiction, seeking all damages permitted under the DTPA, including treble damages and attorneys\' fees.

**[VI. RESERVATION OF RIGHTS]{.underline}**

This letter is without prejudice to any and all rights, remedies, and claims available to Arcadia under the DTPA, the MSLSA, common law, or any other applicable law, all of which are expressly reserved. Nothing in this letter shall be deemed a waiver of any claim, defense, or right of Arcadia, nor shall this letter constitute an election of remedies.

Arcadia further reserves the right to assert claims for breach of contract, fraud, negligent misrepresentation, unjust enrichment, and any other causes of action arising from the facts described herein, in addition to the DTPA claims set forth above.

Please direct all responses and communications regarding this matter to the undersigned at the address listed above.

Very truly yours,

WEXFORD HALE LLP

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Jonathan Breckenridge Partner

Enclosures:

> • Copy of MSLSA (dated March 15, 2022)
>
> • Copy of SOW-1 (dated March 15, 2022)
>
> • Copies of Change Orders CO-001 through CO-004

cc: Dr. Rachel Okonkwo, Managing Member / CEO, Arcadia Health Systems, LLC Martin Schreiber, CTO, Arcadia Health Systems, LLC

600 Congress Avenue, Suite 2800 Austin, Texas 78701

July 10, 2023

Dear Mr. Breckenridge:

We write on behalf of our client, Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. (\"Meridian\"), in response to your letter dated June 26, 2023, directed to Meridian\'s General Counsel, Patricia M. Espinoza, in which you assert various claims on behalf of your client, Arcadia Health Systems, LLC (\"Arcadia\"), arising from the Master Software License and Services Agreement executed on March 15, 2022 (the \"MSLSA\"), and the related Statement of Work #1 (\"SOW-1\"). result:
**STONEBRIDGE & CALLOWAY LLP** Attorneys at Law

600 Congress Avenue, Suite 2800 Austin, Texas 78701

Telephone: (512) 555-7400 Facsimile: (512) 555-7401

July 10, 2023

VIA EMAIL AND CERTIFIED MAIL --- RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED

Jonathan Breckenridge, Esq. Wexford Hale LLP 300 West 6th Street, Suite 1500 Austin, Texas 78701

> Re: Arcadia Health Systems, LLC --- Demand and Threatened Litigation Against Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.; Master Software License and Services Agreement dated March 15, 2022

Dear Mr. Breckenridge:

We write on behalf of our client, Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. (\"Meridian\"), in response to your letter dated June 26, 2023, directed to Meridian\'s General Counsel, Patricia M. Espinoza, in which you assert various claims on behalf of your client, Arcadia Health Systems, LLC (\"Arcadia\"), arising from the Master Software License and Services Agreement executed on March 15, 2022 (the \"MSLSA\"), and the related Statement of Work #1 (\"SOW-1\"). Your letter demands payment of \$47.3 million in alleged damages and threatens litigation if Meridian does not accede to Arcadia\'s demands within thirty days.

We have conducted a thorough review of the MSLSA, SOW-1, all four executed Change Orders (CO-001 through CO-004), the contemporaneous project documentation, Meridian\'s internal records, and the relevant correspondence between the parties. Based on that review, Meridian categorically rejects each of Arcadia\'s asserted claims and demands. This letter sets forth the principal bases for Meridian\'s position. Nothing in this letter should be construed as a complete statement of Meridian\'s defenses, all of which are expressly reserved.

**[I. Arcadia\'s Breach of Contract Claim Is Without Merit]{.underline}**

Your letter alleges that Meridian breached the MSLSA by delivering defective software and failing to achieve timely implementation. These allegations fundamentally mischaracterize the contractual record.

**The Go-Live delay was attributable to Arcadia\'s own failures.** Meridian\'s project status report dated August 25, 2022 --- which was delivered to Arcadia contemporaneously and was never disputed --- identified three root causes for the delay beyond the September 1, 2022 target Go-Live date: (1) Arcadia\'s 45-day gap in designating a Project Manager after Kevin Liu\'s departure on April 28, 2022, in contravention of Section 4 of SOW-1; (2) Arcadia\'s failure to provide complete API specifications until July 20, 2022, which was 75 days past the milestone date established in the SOW-1 project schedule; and (3) critical errors in the data migration scripts prepared by Linden Park Consulting, LLC (\"Linden Park\"), the third-party systems integrator that Arcadia --- not Meridian --- independently retained. Section 3.2 of SOW-1 expressly provides that Arcadia \"shall be solely responsible for all data migration activities, including extraction, transformation, and loading of legacy data into NexusCore\" and that Meridian\'s role was limited to \"advisory consulting services.\" Your client cannot attribute implementation delays caused by its own systems integrator\'s deficient work product to Meridian.

**Arcadia accepted the software.** Under Section 5.3 of the MSLSA, Arcadia had 30 days following deployment of each module to conduct acceptance testing and deliver written notice of any material nonconformity. The NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module achieved Go-Live on January 15, 2023, and the acceptance testing window closed on February 14, 2023. Arcadia did not deliver any written notice of nonconformity within that period. Accordingly, acceptance was deemed granted under the express terms of Section 5.3. Dr. Okonkwo\'s email to Sandra Bledsoe dated March 8, 2023 --- which referenced only \"intermittent latency issues\" and \"occasional report generation errors\" --- was sent 22 days after the acceptance window closed and does not satisfy the contractual notice requirement.

**The limited warranty has expired and Meridian performed its cure obligations.** Section 9.1 of the MSLSA provides a limited warranty that the software will \"perform substantially in accordance with the Documentation\" for 90 days following Go-Live. That 90-day period expired on April 15, 2023. During the warranty period, Meridian\'s support team diligently addressed all reported issues. Of the 47 total support tickets Arcadia submitted between March and July 2023, 31 were resolved within the applicable SLA timeframes, 12 were traceable to data quality issues caused by the Linden Park migration (for which Meridian bore no contractual responsibility), and the remaining 4 involved software defects that Meridian patched through software updates released on May 2, 2023, and June 19, 2023. This performance was fully consistent with --- and indeed exceeded --- Meridian\'s obligations under the MSLSA.

**The damages sought are barred by the MSLSA\'s limitation of liability provisions.** Even if Arcadia could establish a breach (which it cannot), the MSLSA contains unambiguous limitations on recoverable damages. Section 8.1 caps either party\'s aggregate liability at \"the total fees paid or payable by Licensee during the twelve (12) month period immediately preceding the event giving rise to such liability.\" Section 8.2 expressly excludes liability for any indirect, incidental, special, consequential, punitive, or exemplary damages, including lost profits, goodwill, and reputational harm. Arcadia\'s \$47.3 million demand --- comprising \$18.4 million in lost profits, \$6.7 million in increased operating costs, and \$5.0 million in purported reputational harm --- is overwhelmingly composed of consequential damages that are categorically excluded by the parties\' agreement.

**[II. Arcadia\'s Fraud and Negligent Misrepresentation Claims Fail as a Matter of Law]{.underline}**

Your letter alleges that Meridian, through its Account Executive James Poletti, made fraudulent representations during the pre-sale process regarding NexusCore\'s capabilities and integration functionality. These claims are legally deficient for multiple independent reasons.

**The integration clause bars reliance on extra-contractual representations.** Section 12.1 of the MSLSA provides that the Agreement \"constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior and contemporaneous agreements, proposals, representations, and understandings, whether oral or written.\" Under Delaware law, which governs the MSLSA pursuant to Section 12.7, a merger clause of this breadth is enforceable and precludes claims based on alleged pre-contractual representations that were not incorporated into the written agreement. *See Abry Partners V, L.P. v. F&W Acquisition LLC*, 891 A.2d 1032, 1059 (Del. Ch. 2006).

**The alleged misrepresentations are non-actionable puffery or forward-looking statements.** The sales presentation materials referenced general marketing language --- \"industry-leading performance\" and \"30-40% improvement in reporting efficiency\" --- which constitutes classic puffery that is not actionable as fraud under either Delaware or Texas law. Moreover, Meridian\'s December 9, 2021 written proposal contained express disclaimers that \"\[e\]stimated timelines and performance metrics are provided for planning purposes only and do not constitute guarantees.\"

**Arcadia cannot establish justifiable reliance.** Arcadia is a sophisticated commercial entity with approximately \$62 million in annual revenue, represented by experienced outside counsel during the negotiation of the MSLSA. Arcadia\'s own CTO, Martin Schreiber, conducted independent due diligence --- including reference calls with two existing Meridian healthcare clients and a two-week proof-of-concept trial --- before contract execution. Mr. Schreiber\'s own internal memorandum acknowledged that \"\[i\]ntegration complexity is manageable but will require a skilled SI partner.\" This conclusively demonstrates that Arcadia made its purchasing decision based on its own independent investigation, not on any alleged representations by Mr. Poletti. Any claim of justifiable reliance on general sales statements in the face of this independent diligence and a comprehensive integration clause fails as a matter of law.

**[III. Arcadia\'s Unjust Enrichment Claim Is Precluded by the MSLSA]{.underline}**

Arcadia\'s unjust enrichment claim is barred because a valid, enforceable contract --- the MSLSA --- governs the subject matter of the dispute. Under both Delaware and Texas law, an unjust enrichment claim cannot be maintained where an express contract covers the same subject matter. *See Nemec v. Shrader*, 991 A.2d 1120, 1130 (Del. 2010); *Fortis Benefits v. Cantu*, 234 S.W.3d 642, 649 (Tex. 2007). Arcadia does not and cannot allege that the MSLSA is void or unenforceable. Accordingly, this claim must be abandoned.

**[IV. Arcadia\'s DTPA Claim Is Barred by the Section 17.49(f) Exemption and the Consumer Status Requirement]{.underline}**

Your letter asserts a claim under the Texas Deceptive Trade Practices --- Consumer Protection Act, Tex. Bus. & Com. Code Sections 17.41--17.63 (the \"DTPA\"), and demands \$2.5 million in treble damages. This claim is without foundation.

**The DTPA exemption for large transactions applies.** Section 17.49(f) of the Texas Business and Commerce Code exempts from DTPA coverage any \"cause of action arising from a transaction, a project, or a set of transactions relating to the same project, involving total consideration by the consumer of more than \$500,000\" unless the consumer is an individual. Arcadia is a Texas limited liability company, not an individual. The total consideration under the MSLSA alone was \$14,700,000, and the total consideration including the four Change Orders was \$17,050,000 --- more than thirty-four times the statutory threshold. The exemption plainly applies, and Arcadia\'s DTPA claim is barred.

We note that Section 17.49(f) also provides an exemption where the defendant\'s \"assets at the time of the act or practice complained of\" exceed \$25 million. To preserve our client\'s rights and fully evaluate all applicable exemptions, **we hereby request that Arcadia produce documentation of its total assets as of March 15, 2022, including but not limited to audited or reviewed financial statements, balance sheets, and tax returns**. To the extent Arcadia\'s total assets exceeded \$25 million at the relevant time --- which we believe likely given Arcadia\'s reported annual revenues of approximately \$62 million and its operations spanning 23 hospitals and over 140 clinics --- an additional basis for the Section 17.49(f) exemption exists.

**Arcadia does not qualify as a \"consumer\" under the DTPA.** Even if the Section 17.49(f) exemption were somehow inapplicable, the DTPA protects only a \"consumer,\" defined under Section 17.45(4) as \"an individual, partnership, corporation, this state, or a subdivision or agency of this state who seeks or acquires by purchase or lease, any goods or services.\" Texas courts have consistently held that to qualify as a \"consumer,\" the claimant must have sought or acquired goods or services, and the transaction must have a consumer, rather than purely commercial, character. The MSLSA is a sophisticated enterprise software licensing agreement negotiated at arm\'s length between two commercial entities, each represented by counsel, with a total contract value of \$14.7 million. This is not the type of consumer transaction the DTPA was enacted to protect. *See Riverside Nat\'l Bank v. Lewis*, 603 S.W.2d 169, 174--75 (Tex. 1980) (analyzing consumer status under the DTPA).

**[V. Reservation of Rights]{.underline}**

Meridian reserves all rights, remedies, claims, and defenses available to it under the MSLSA, at law, and in equity, including but not limited to all affirmative defenses, rights under the contractual limitation of liability and consequential damages waiver provisions, and any counterclaims arising from Arcadia\'s own material breaches of the MSLSA and SOW-1. Nothing in this letter constitutes a waiver of any such right or defense, nor an admission of any fact or legal theory.

Meridian encourages Arcadia to carefully reconsider the merits of its threatened claims in light of the foregoing. Should Arcadia wish to discuss a practical resolution of any outstanding operational issues with the NexusCore platform, Meridian remains willing to engage in good-faith discussions regarding ongoing support, subject to the terms of the MSLSA. However, Meridian will not entertain demands for payment grounded in meritless legal theories.

If Arcadia chooses to pursue litigation notwithstanding this response, Meridian will defend vigorously and will seek recovery of its attorneys\' fees and costs to the fullest extent permitted by law and the MSLSA.

Please direct all further correspondence regarding this matter to the undersigned.

Very truly yours,

STONEBRIDGE & CALLOWAY LLP

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Margaret \"Meg\" Calloway Lead Partner

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

David Arsenault Senior Associate

cc: Patricia M. Espinoza, General Counsel, Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.

This letter and any attachments are confidential, may be protected by the attorney-client privilege and/or work product doctrine, and are intended solely for the use of the addressee. If you have received this communication in error, please notify the sender immediately and destroy all copies.

**ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC** **INTERNAL MEMORANDUM --- CONFIDENTIAL**

**TO:** Dr. Rachel Okonkwo, Managing Member / CEO **FROM:** Martin Schreiber, Chief Technology Officer **DATE:** January 19, 2022 **RE:** NexusCore Platform --- Due Diligence Findings and Recommendation

**[1. Executive Summary]{.underline}**

Over the past several weeks, I have conducted an independent technical and operational due diligence review of Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.\'s NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module as a potential replacement for our legacy HealthBridge 4.0 platform. This review included reference calls with two existing Meridian healthcare clients, a two-week proof-of-concept (\"POC\") trial conducted in our San Antonio test environment, and an assessment of NexusCore\'s architecture, API framework, and reporting capabilities.

My overall assessment is positive. **NexusCore appears well-suited to our needs. Integration complexity is manageable but will require a skilled SI partner.** The platform\'s modular architecture, cloud-native design, and healthcare-specific analytics tooling represent a meaningful upgrade from HealthBridge 4.0. However, there are integration considerations --- particularly around data migration and EHR connectivity across our 23 hospitals and 140+ clinic sites --- that we must plan for carefully. I recommend we proceed to contract negotiations with Meridian, subject to the conditions outlined in Section 6 below.

**[2. Reference Calls]{.underline}**

I conducted reference calls with technology leadership at two existing Meridian healthcare clients. Both organizations agreed to speak candidly on the condition that their feedback remain confidential. Below is a summary of each conversation.

**2.1 San Marcos Regional Health**

**Contact:** David Faulkner, Vice President of Information Systems **Date of Call:** January 6, 2022 **Duration:** Approximately 45 minutes

San Marcos Regional Health (\"SMRH\") is a mid-sized regional health system operating seven hospitals and approximately 60 affiliated clinics in the greater San Marcos area. SMRH deployed NexusCore\'s Healthcare Analytics Module in Q2 2021 and has been live on the platform for approximately seven months at the time of our call.

Mr. Faulkner reported that the implementation took approximately five months from kickoff to go-live, which he described as \"on the longer side of what Meridian originally estimated, but within the range we expected given our environment.\" He noted that the primary driver of timeline extension was SMRH\'s own internal resource constraints during the configuration phase, not deficiencies on Meridian\'s side. He specifically praised Meridian\'s project management team and stated that Meridian\'s technical consultants were \"responsive and knowledgeable.\"

Regarding software performance, Faulkner indicated that NexusCore\'s reporting engine is \"significantly faster\" than their prior analytics platform and that the healthcare-specific data models reduced the amount of custom configuration they needed. He noted occasional latency during peak usage hours in the first two months post-go-live, but said Meridian addressed the issue through a server-side optimization within approximately three weeks of their support ticket.

On the subject of data migration, Faulkner offered an important caution. SMRH initially attempted to handle data migration internally and encountered \"serious data integrity issues\" that delayed their go-live by approximately three weeks. They ultimately brought in an outside systems integrator to remediate the migration scripts and complete the data load. Faulkner stated: \"If I had to do it over again, I would have engaged an experienced SI partner for the migration from day one. That is not Meridian\'s core competency, and they are upfront about that.\"

**Overall assessment from SMRH: Positive.** Faulkner said he would recommend NexusCore and stated that SMRH is evaluating an expansion of its Meridian deployment to include additional modules.

**2.2 Coastal Bend Medical Group**

**Contact:** Anita Suresh, Chief Information Officer **Date of Call:** January 10, 2022 **Duration:** Approximately 35 minutes

Coastal Bend Medical Group (\"CBMG\") is a large multi-specialty physician group operating 42 clinic locations across the Corpus Christi metropolitan area. CBMG deployed NexusCore in Q4 2020 and has been operating on the platform for over a year.

Ms. Suresh was broadly positive about the NexusCore platform. She highlighted the analytics dashboards as a \"game-changer\" for CBMG\'s value-based care reporting and noted that NexusCore\'s standard API connectors worked well with their primary EHR system. However, she noted that CBMG\'s environment involved only a single EHR platform, and she cautioned that organizations running multiple EHR systems would face \"a different level of integration complexity.\" She recommended building ample time into the project plan for API configuration and testing in multi-EHR environments.

Suresh also emphasized the importance of data quality in the migration process. She stated that CBMG invested approximately six weeks in data cleansing and validation prior to migration and that this investment \"paid for itself many times over\" by avoiding post-go-live data quality issues. She observed that organizations that underinvest in data preparation tend to attribute resulting data quality problems to the new platform rather than to the underlying data.

When asked about Meridian\'s support organization, Suresh rated it \"above average\" and noted that their assigned customer success manager was proactive in scheduling quarterly business reviews. She mentioned one frustration --- that Meridian\'s standard reporting templates required \"more customization than the sales team led us to believe\" --- but characterized this as a \"manageable issue\" that was resolved through their implementation services engagement.

**Overall assessment from CBMG: Positive with caveats.** Suresh recommended NexusCore but stressed the need for realistic expectations around integration timelines in complex environments.

**[3. Proof-of-Concept Trial]{.underline}**

**3.1 Trial Parameters**

We conducted a two-week proof-of-concept trial of the NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module from January 4 through January 18, 2022. Meridian provisioned a dedicated cloud instance and loaded a de-identified sample dataset that we provided, representing approximately 180,000 patient records drawn from three of our hospital sites. The POC environment included NexusCore\'s standard healthcare analytics dashboards, reporting engine, and API sandbox.

Our evaluation team consisted of myself, Kevin Liu (Project Manager), two senior data engineers from our analytics team, and one clinical informatics specialist.

**3.2 Functional Evaluation**

The POC trial confirmed that NexusCore\'s core analytics capabilities are robust and represent a substantial improvement over HealthBridge 4.0. Specific findings include:

> • **Reporting Performance:** Report generation times averaged 3.2 seconds for standard dashboards and 11.7 seconds for complex ad hoc queries against the 180,000-record dataset. By comparison, equivalent reports in HealthBridge 4.0 average 14--18 seconds and 45+ seconds, respectively. This performance improvement is meaningful for our operational users.
>
> • **Healthcare Data Models:** NexusCore\'s pre-built healthcare data models (including FHIR-aligned structures, ICD-10 mapping tables, and CMS quality measure templates) are comprehensive and well-documented. Our clinical informatics specialist confirmed that the models align with our reporting requirements for value-based care contracts.
>
> • **User Interface:** The dashboard interface is modern, intuitive, and configurable. Our team required minimal training to navigate the POC environment. The drag-and-drop report builder is a significant upgrade from HealthBridge 4.0\'s manual query interface.
>
> • **API Framework:** We tested NexusCore\'s standard API connectors against a simulated EHR endpoint. The connectors functioned as documented for standard HL7 FHIR R4 transactions. However, our team noted that the standard connectors do not natively support several legacy data exchange formats that some of our older clinic-based EHR systems still use. Meridian\'s pre-sales engineer, during a follow-up technical call, confirmed that custom API connectors would be required for these legacy systems and that this would need to be scoped as part of the implementation services engagement.

**3.3 Identified Risks and Limitations**

The POC trial also revealed several areas of concern that must be addressed in implementation planning:

1\. **Multi-EHR Integration Complexity:** Our environment includes five distinct EHR platforms across our 23 hospitals and 140+ clinics. The POC tested connectivity with only one EHR system. Integrating with all five platforms --- particularly the two legacy systems still running non-FHIR-compliant interfaces --- will be a significant workstream. This aligns with Ms. Suresh\'s caution from the CBMG reference call. I estimate this will require dedicated API development and extensive testing cycles.

2\. **Data Migration Scale:** Our HealthBridge 4.0 environment contains approximately 12.8 million patient records, 340 million clinical data points, and over seven years of historical reporting data. The POC used only a 180,000-record sample. Migrating the full production dataset will be an order-of-magnitude more complex undertaking. Both reference clients emphasized that data migration was the single most challenging aspect of their NexusCore deployments. Based on what I have learned, **I strongly recommend that we engage an experienced, independent systems integration firm to manage the data migration workstream.** This should not be treated as a task our internal team handles on the side.

3\. **Customization Requirements:** While NexusCore\'s standard reporting templates cover approximately 70--75% of our current reporting needs, the remaining 25--30% will require custom report development. Some of our contractual reporting obligations with managed care payors involve non-standard data formats and calculations that are specific to our organization. This customization work should be scoped explicitly in any Statement of Work.

4\. **Change Management:** The shift from HealthBridge 4.0 to NexusCore will require significant change management across our clinical and operational staff. NexusCore\'s interface paradigm is fundamentally different from HealthBridge 4.0, and we should budget for an extended training and adoption period. I recommend we plan for at least 12--16 weeks of parallel operation during the transition.

**[4. Vendor Financial and Operational Assessment]{.underline}**

Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. is a publicly traded company (NASDAQ: MCSI) with reported annual revenue of approximately \$380 million. I reviewed their most recent publicly available financial filings and confirmed that the company appears financially stable, with consistent revenue growth and positive operating cash flow. Their customer base includes several prominent healthcare organizations, and the company has invested in dedicated healthcare vertical product development over the past three years.

I see no material vendor viability risk at this time.

**[5. Comparison to Alternatives]{.underline}**

For completeness, I note that we also evaluated two alternative platforms earlier in our vendor selection process: MedAnalytica Cloud (from Hargrove Technologies) and InsightRx Enterprise (from Cascade Data Systems). NexusCore outperformed both alternatives in our weighted scoring matrix across the categories of healthcare-specific functionality, reporting speed, API extensibility, and total cost of ownership over a three-year period. The primary advantage NexusCore holds over the alternatives is its pre-built healthcare data models, which significantly reduce configuration time and ongoing maintenance burden.

**[6. Recommendations and Conditions for Proceeding]{.underline}**

Based on my due diligence review, I recommend that we proceed to contract negotiations with Meridian Cloud Solutions for the NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module, subject to the following conditions:

1\. **Retain a Qualified Systems Integrator.** We must engage a reputable, experienced systems integration partner to manage the data migration workstream. This is not optional. The reference calls and POC trial both confirmed that data migration is the highest-risk element of this project, and Meridian itself does not position data migration as a service it provides beyond advisory consulting. I recommend we begin evaluating SI candidates immediately so that we can have a partner identified and under contract by the time implementation begins.

2\. **Negotiate Detailed Statement of Work.** The SOW must clearly delineate responsibilities between Meridian, our internal team, and the SI partner. In particular, API integration responsibilities, acceptance testing criteria, and data migration accountability must be explicitly allocated. Ambiguity here will create risk.

3\. **Allocate Adequate Internal Resources.** We will need to designate a dedicated full-time Project Manager to serve as our single point of contact with Meridian throughout the implementation. This person must have decision-making authority and deep knowledge of our systems environment. I recommend Kevin Liu for this role. We will also need to commit at least two senior data engineers and one clinical informatics specialist to the project on a half-time basis during the implementation phase.

4\. **Budget for Contingency.** Based on the reference calls and my experience with enterprise software deployments of this scale, I recommend building a 15--20% cost contingency into our implementation budget to cover scope adjustments, unforeseen integration challenges, and additional customization needs. Enterprise ERP deployments of this nature rarely come in exactly at initial estimates.

5\. **Engage Outside Counsel for Contract Review.** Given the contract value and the complexity of the implementation, I recommend that we engage outside counsel to review and negotiate the MSLSA. Particular attention should be given to limitation of liability provisions, warranty terms, acceptance testing procedures, and the allocation of responsibility between licensor and licensee.

**[7. Conclusion]{.underline}**

NexusCore is a strong platform that addresses our core requirements and represents a generational improvement over HealthBridge 4.0. The risks I have identified --- multi-EHR integration complexity, data migration scale, and customization needs --- are manageable with proper planning, adequate resourcing, and a skilled systems integration partner. These are execution risks, not platform risks. I am confident that with the right implementation approach, NexusCore can deliver meaningful value to Arcadia.

I am available to discuss these findings at your convenience and recommend we schedule a leadership review meeting within the next two weeks to make a formal go/no-go decision.

Martin Schreiber Chief Technology Officer Arcadia Health Systems, LLC 4500 Medical Parkway, Suite 210 San Antonio, TX 78229 mschreiber@arcadiahealth.com

*This memorandum is confidential and intended solely for the internal use of Arcadia Health Systems, LLC leadership. Distribution outside the organization is prohibited without the prior written consent of the CTO.*

**From:** Rachel Okonkwo \<rokonkwo@arcadiahealth.com\> **To:** Sandra Bledsoe \<sbledsoe@meridiancloud.com\> **Date:** March 8, 2023, 2:47 PM CST **Subject:** NexusCore --- Performance Items for Your Team\'s Attention **CC:** Martin Schreiber \<mschreiber@arcadiahealth.com\>

Sandra,

I hope this note finds you well. Thank you again for your team\'s efforts in getting us through Go-Live in January --- I know the extended timeline was not easy on either side, and we appreciate the dedication your implementation group showed in the final push.

I am reaching out because our clinical informatics team has flagged a couple of items over the past few weeks that I wanted to bring to your attention so we can get them on your support team\'s radar.

First, we have been experiencing **intermittent latency issues** when running population-health queries against data sets exceeding roughly 500,000 patient records. The delays are inconsistent --- some queries return in the expected timeframe while others hang for upwards of four to five minutes on what should be comparable workloads. Our internal IT group has verified that our network throughput and cloud instance configurations are within the specifications outlined in the deployment guide, so we believe the issue may sit on the application side.

Second, several of our hospital analytics coordinators have reported **occasional report generation errors** in the scheduled overnight batch runs. Specifically, the pre-built readmission-risk reports are intermittently returning incomplete result sets or timing out entirely. We have not yet been able to identify a reliable pattern --- it seems to occur roughly two to three times per week across different facility nodes.

Neither of these issues has caused any patient-safety concerns, and our teams have been working around them, but I would love to get your support engineers looped in so we can troubleshoot before the volume picks up heading into Q2. If it would be helpful, Martin Schreiber on our side can coordinate access to our logging environment and schedule a joint diagnostic call at your team\'s convenience.

Please let me know the best way to get tickets opened for these items and whether you would prefer we route through the standard support portal or handle directly given the nature of the questions.

Looking forward to hearing from you.

Warm regards,

**Dr. Rachel Okonkwo** Managing Member & CEO Arcadia Health Systems, LLC 4500 Medical Parkway, Suite 210 San Antonio, TX 78229 (210) 555-4100 rokonkwo@arcadiahealth.com

text: This is very valuable. The Schreiber internal memo (Jan 19, 2022) is powerful defense evidence — it shows Arcadia knew about integration complexity and data migration risks before signing, and recommended engaging an SI partner and outside counsel. The Okonkwo email (March 8, 2023) is the first written complaint, 22 days after the acceptance window closed, and is measured in tone. I also note a potential DTPA 60-day notice issue (demand dated June 15, 2023; suit filed August 7, 2023 — only 53 days). Let me read the remaining key documents: the written proposal (disclaimers), the project status report, the support ticket summary, the change orders, and the contract negotiation email chain.
