>
> • Enabled real-time performance benchmarking across all 12 clinic locations

**Client Testimonial.** *\"Before NexusCore, our leadership team was making decisions based on data that was weeks or months old. Now we have real-time visibility across every clinic, and it has transformed how we manage the business.\"* --- Chief Operating Officer, Coastal Bend Medical Group

**PRESENTER NOTES --- SLIDE 13 (COASTAL BEND CASE STUDY):** Second case study --- a smaller organization than San Marcos, but relevant because it demonstrates NexusCore\'s applicability across different organizational sizes and structures. Coastal Bend is an ambulatory-focused group, which may resonate with the clinic-side operations within Arcadia\'s network (140+ clinics). Again, results are self-reported and specific to this client\'s experience. Available for reference call. Note that Coastal Bend\'s implementation was completed in approximately 5 months, which is within the typical range for a deployment of that scope.

**[SLIDE 14 --- PLATFORM SECURITY & COMPLIANCE]{.underline}**

**Enterprise-Grade Security.**

  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Security Domain**                 **Capability**
  ----------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Data Encryption**                 AES-256 encryption at rest; TLS 1.3 encryption in transit

  **Access Control**                  Role-based access control (RBAC), multi-factor authentication (MFA), single sign-on (SSO)

  **Audit Logging**                   Comprehensive audit trail of all user actions, data access, and system events

  **Network Security**                Web application firewall (WAF), intrusion detection/prevention (IDS/IPS), DDoS mitigation

  **Disaster Recovery**               Geo-redundant backup, RPO \< 1 hour, RTO \< 4 hours

  **Vulnerability Management**        Quarterly penetration testing, continuous vulnerability scanning, responsible disclosure program
  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Compliance Certifications.**

> • **SOC 2 Type II** --- Annual examination covering security, availability, and confidentiality trust service criteria
>
> • **HITRUST CSF** --- Certified across all in-scope control domains
>
> • **HIPAA** --- Full BAA execution; administrative, physical, and technical safeguards implemented
>
> • **NIST Cybersecurity Framework** --- Aligned to NIST CSF core functions

**Data Residency.**

> • All client data hosted in Tier 1 U.S.-based data centers
>
> • No offshore data processing or storage
>
> • Client data logically segregated in multi-tenant architecture

**PRESENTER NOTES --- SLIDE 14 (SECURITY & COMPLIANCE):** Healthcare organizations have stringent security and compliance requirements. Walk through the security architecture at a high level and emphasize the compliance certifications. Offer to provide detailed security documentation, including our SOC 2 Type II report and HITRUST certification letter, under NDA. Note that Meridian executes a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with all healthcare clients as standard practice. If Arcadia has specific security questionnaire requirements, we can coordinate completion through our InfoSec team.

**[SLIDE 15 --- PRICING OVERVIEW]{.underline}**

**Flexible Licensing & Services Model**

NexusCore is priced on a modular basis, allowing clients to invest in only the capabilities they need.

**Pricing Components.**

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                 **Description**                                       **Pricing Basis**
  ----------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------
  **Software License**          Right to access and use NexusCore modules             Annual subscription or multi-year term license

  **Implementation Services**   Configuration, customization, integration, training   Fixed-fee based on Statement of Work scope

  **Maintenance & Support**     Software updates, bug fixes, technical support        Annual fee (percentage of license)

  **Training**                  End-user and administrator training programs          Per-session or bundled package

  **Advisory Services**         Data migration consulting, optimization consulting    Time-and-materials or fixed-fee
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Pricing Principles.**

> • All pricing is customized based on organizational size, module selection, and implementation scope
>
> • Multi-year term licenses offer favorable pricing compared to annual subscriptions
>
> • Pricing does not include third-party costs (e.g., systems integrator fees, infrastructure, internal resource allocation)

**Investment Range (Indicative)**

For an organization of Arcadia\'s size and scope (20+ hospitals, 100+ clinics, healthcare analytics module):

> • **Estimated Total Investment:** \$12,000,000 -- \$18,000,000 over a 3-year term
>
> • Includes software license, implementation, Year 1 maintenance, training, and advisory services
>
> • Excludes third-party systems integrator fees and client internal costs

*Detailed pricing will be provided in a formal written proposal following this presentation.*

**PRESENTER NOTES --- SLIDE 15 (PRICING):** Provide indicative pricing range only. Do not commit to specific numbers at this stage --- the formal proposal will follow after we complete a more detailed scoping exercise. The \$12M--\$18M range is based on comparable deployments for organizations of similar size. Actual pricing will depend on specific module selection, customization requirements, implementation scope, and contract term. Emphasize that this range excludes third-party costs --- in particular, the cost of a systems integrator for data migration, which can be significant depending on the complexity of the legacy environment. We will provide a detailed written proposal with line-item pricing.

**[SLIDE 16 --- WHY NEXUSCORE]{.underline}**

**Five Reasons to Choose NexusCore for Healthcare Analytics**

**1. Purpose-Built for Healthcare**

> • Deep healthcare domain expertise embedded in the platform
>
> • 200+ healthcare organizations rely on NexusCore today
>
> • Continuous investment in healthcare-specific features and regulatory updates

**2. Proven Integration Capabilities**

> • Pre-built connectors for all major EHR platforms
>
> • Standard API framework enables connectivity with virtually any data source
>
> • Dedicated integration engineering team with healthcare interoperability expertise

**3. Scalable Architecture**

> • Cloud-native platform scales with your organization\'s growth
>
> • Modular design means you deploy what you need, when you need it
>
> • Supports organizations from single facilities to multi-state health systems

**4. Commitment to Client Success**

> • Dedicated Customer Success Manager assigned to every account
>
> • 94% annual client retention rate across healthcare vertical
>
> • Structured implementation methodology with defined milestones and shared accountability

**5. Continuous Innovation**

> • Quarterly platform releases with new features and enhancements
>
> • Active client advisory board that shapes the product roadmap
>
> • Investment in machine learning and predictive analytics capabilities

**PRESENTER NOTES --- SLIDE 16 (WHY NEXUSCORE):** Summary value proposition. This slide should reinforce the key themes of the presentation. Tailor the discussion to the priorities Arcadia has expressed during the session. If integration is their primary concern, spend more time on point 2. If scalability matters most, emphasize point 3. The \"200+ healthcare organizations\" figure is current as of September 30, 2021. The 94% retention rate is averaged across FY 2020 and FY 2021. These are factual statements about our client base and retention, respectively. They are not predictions of Arcadia\'s future experience.

**[SLIDE 17 --- PROPOSED NEXT STEPS]{.underline}**

**Recommended Path Forward**

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Step**                        **Timeline**                       **Description**
  ------------------------------- ---------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **1. Technical Deep-Dive**      Week of Oct 25, 2021               Detailed technical session with Arcadia\'s IT team to review system landscape, integration requirements, and data migration considerations

  **2. Reference Calls**          Weeks of Nov 1--12, 2021           Arrange reference calls with San Marcos Regional Health and Coastal Bend Medical Group

  **3. Proof-of-Concept Trial**   Nov 15 -- Dec 10, 2021 (approx.)   2--4 week POC trial using sample data in a dedicated demo environment

  **4. Written Proposal**         Week of Dec 6, 2021                Deliver formal written proposal with detailed scope, pricing, and timeline

  **5. Contract Negotiation**     January 2022                       Commercial and legal review of Master Software License and Services Agreement

  **6. Project Kickoff**          Target: March 2022                 Requirements gathering phase commences
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Your Meridian Team**

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Role**                   **Name**                         **Contact**
  -------------------------- -------------------------------- -----------------------------
  Account Executive          James Poletti                    j.poletti@meridiancloud.com

  Solutions Architect        (To be assigned)                 ---

  Customer Success Manager   (To be assigned post-contract)   ---
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**PRESENTER NOTES --- SLIDE 17 (NEXT STEPS):** Outline the proposed next steps and confirm Arcadia\'s interest in proceeding. The timeline above is suggested and flexible. Key action items: (1) Schedule the technical deep-dive session with Arcadia\'s CTO, Martin Schreiber, and IT team. (2) Coordinate reference calls --- check availability with San Marcos and Coastal Bend contacts. (3) Set up POC environment and work with Arcadia to define POC success criteria. Confirm that Arcadia is comfortable with the general direction before investing time in the detailed technical sessions. Ask whether Arcadia has engaged outside counsel for contract review and whether they have a preferred timeline for the procurement process.

**[SLIDE 18 --- FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS]{.underline}**

**Q: How long does a typical implementation take?** A: For an organization of Arcadia\'s size deploying the Healthcare Analytics Module, a typical implementation takes 5--7 months from contract execution to Go-Live. Actual timelines depend on scope, client readiness, and resource availability.

**Q: Can NexusCore integrate with our existing EHR systems?** A: NexusCore is designed to integrate with major EHR platforms through our standard API framework. We offer pre-built connectors for Epic, Cerner, MEDITECH, Allscripts, athenahealth, and others. Custom integrations are available for legacy or niche systems through professional services.

**Q: Who is responsible for data migration?** A: Data migration is the client\'s responsibility. Meridian provides advisory consulting services --- including migration planning, data mapping guidance, and target schema documentation --- but the actual extraction, transformation, and loading of data is performed by the client or the client\'s designated systems integrator. We strongly recommend engaging a qualified SI partner.

**Q: What happens if we encounter issues after Go-Live?** A: Meridian provides a post-Go-Live stabilization period (typically 2--4 weeks) with enhanced support. Ongoing maintenance and support includes software updates, bug fixes, and technical support per SLA. A dedicated Customer Success Manager is assigned to every account.

**Q: What training is included?** A: Training is provided as part of the implementation engagement and can be customized to the client\'s needs. Options include end-user training, administrator training, and train-the-trainer programs.

**PRESENTER NOTES --- SLIDE 18 (FAQ):** Anticipate common questions. If Arcadia raises questions not covered here, note them for follow-up. The data migration question is particularly important --- reinforce the shared responsibility model and the recommendation to engage a qualified systems integrator. If asked about specific guarantees of performance or outcomes, redirect to the written proposal and contractual documentation, which will contain the definitive terms.

**[SLIDE 19 --- Q&A]{.underline}**

*Thank you for your time and attention.*

*We welcome your questions.*

**Contact Information. James Poletti** Account Executive, Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. Email: j.poletti@meridiancloud.com Phone: (512) 555-0147

**Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.** 9200 Research Boulevard, Suite 400 Austin, TX 78759 www.meridiancloud.com

**PRESENTER NOTES --- SLIDE 19 (Q&A):** Open the floor for questions. Allow 10--15 minutes for discussion. Be prepared to address questions about competitive positioning, technical architecture details, pricing, and contract terms. For any question that requires detailed technical analysis, offer to follow up with a written response or schedule a dedicated working session. Before closing, confirm the next step: scheduling the technical deep-dive session with Martin Schreiber\'s team, targeted for the week of October 25, 2021.

**[SLIDE 20 --- IMPORTANT NOTICES AND DISCLAIMERS]{.underline}**

**DISCLAIMER**

This presentation is for informational purposes only and does not constitute a warranty or guarantee of performance. The information contained herein is provided to facilitate preliminary discussions between Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. (\"Meridian\") and the intended recipient and is subject to change without notice.

All statements regarding product capabilities, features, and performance are general descriptions and do not represent binding commitments. Any warranties applicable to Meridian\'s products or services will be solely those set forth in a definitive written agreement executed by authorized representatives of both parties.

**FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS.** This presentation may contain forward-looking statements within the meaning of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995, including statements regarding product features, capabilities, roadmap, and market positioning. These statements are based on current expectations and assumptions and are subject to risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results to differ materially. Meridian undertakes no obligation to update any forward-looking statements.

**ESTIMATED PERFORMANCE METRICS.** References to client results, efficiency improvements, cost savings, or other performance metrics are based on self-reported data from existing clients or general industry observations. They represent the experience of specific organizations under specific circumstances and are not projections or guarantees of results for any other organization. Individual results will vary based on numerous factors, including but not limited to organizational readiness, data quality, implementation scope, resource availability, third-party systems integrator performance, and user adoption.

**PRICING.** Any pricing information provided in this presentation is indicative only and does not constitute an offer. Definitive pricing will be set forth in a formal written proposal and, ultimately, in a mutually executed agreement.

**CONFIDENTIALITY.** This presentation and its contents are confidential and proprietary to Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. Distribution or reproduction without Meridian\'s prior written consent is prohibited.

Do not remove or modify this slide without approval from the Meridian Legal Department. **GRAYSON WHITMORE & CO.** *Financial Advisory & Litigation Consulting Services*

1200 Main Street, Suite 3400 Dallas, Texas 75202

**EXECUTIVE SUMMARY** **DAMAGES ANALYSIS**

**Prepared for:** **Arcadia Health Systems, LLC**

**In Connection with Claims Against:** **Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.**

**Case No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA** **U.S. District Court, Western District of Texas, Austin Division**

**July 28, 2023**

CONFIDENTIAL --- This document was prepared by Grayson Whitmore & Co. at the request of Arcadia Health Systems, LLC and its counsel, Wexford Hale LLP, in connection with pending litigation. This executive summary is intended for use by counsel and is subject to all applicable privileges. Distribution is restricted to authorized parties. Nothing herein constitutes a legal opinion.

**[I. INTRODUCTION AND ENGAGEMENT SCOPE]{.underline}**

Grayson Whitmore & Co. (\"GW&Co.\" or the \"Firm\") was retained by Arcadia Health Systems, LLC (\"Arcadia\" or the \"Company\") and its co
result:
**GRAYSON WHITMORE & CO.** *Financial Advisory & Litigation Consulting Services*

1200 Main Street, Suite 3400 Dallas, Texas 75202

**EXECUTIVE SUMMARY** **DAMAGES ANALYSIS**

**Prepared for:** **Arcadia Health Systems, LLC**

**In Connection with Claims Against:** **Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.**

**Case No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA** **U.S. District Court, Western District of Texas, Austin Division**

**July 28, 2023**

CONFIDENTIAL --- This document was prepared by Grayson Whitmore & Co. at the request of Arcadia Health Systems, LLC and its counsel, Wexford Hale LLP, in connection with pending litigation. This executive summary is intended for use by counsel and is subject to all applicable privileges. Distribution is restricted to authorized parties. Nothing herein constitutes a legal opinion.

**[I. INTRODUCTION AND ENGAGEMENT SCOPE]{.underline}**

Grayson Whitmore & Co. (\"GW&Co.\" or the \"Firm\") was retained by Arcadia Health Systems, LLC (\"Arcadia\" or the \"Company\") and its counsel, Wexford Hale LLP, in April 2023 to prepare a comprehensive analysis of the economic damages sustained by Arcadia arising from the failed deployment of Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.\'s (\"Meridian\") NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module under the Master Software License and Services Agreement dated March 15, 2022 (the \"MSLSA\"), and the related Statement of Work #1 (\"SOW-1\").

**Engagement Team.** This analysis was led by Robert Davenport, CFA, CPA/ABV, Managing Director and head of GW&Co.\'s Litigation Consulting Practice, with support from Senior Associates Emily Tran, CPA, and Marcus Hightower, CFA. The engagement team collectively brings over forty-five years of experience in commercial damages quantification, with particular expertise in technology licensing disputes and healthcare industry financial analysis.

**Scope of Work.** GW&Co. was engaged to perform the following:

> \(a\) Quantify direct economic losses resulting from Arcadia\'s payments under the MSLSA and related Change Orders;
>
> \(b\) Project lost profits attributable to the disruption of Arcadia\'s business operations and the loss of prospective client relationships;
>
> \(c\) Calculate increased operating costs incurred by Arcadia as a direct and proximate result of the NexusCore deployment failure;
>
> \(d\) Estimate reputational harm sustained by Arcadia in its relevant market; and
>
> \(e\) Assess the applicability and quantum of statutory damages under the Texas Deceptive Trade Practices Act, Tex. Bus. & Com. Code Sections 17.41--17.63 (the \"DTPA\").

**Information Relied Upon.** In preparing this analysis, GW&Co. reviewed the following materials, among others: the MSLSA and all exhibits and schedules thereto; SOW-1; Change Orders CO-001 through CO-004; Arcadia\'s audited financial statements for fiscal years 2020 through 2022 and unaudited interim financial statements through Q2 2023; Arcadia\'s internal budgets, forecasts, and strategic planning documents; project status reports and correspondence between Arcadia and Meridian; support ticket logs and defect tracking reports; Arcadia\'s client contracts and pipeline reports; market research regarding the Texas and Louisiana healthcare IT services sector; and interviews with Arcadia management, including Dr. Rachel Okonkwo (Managing Member and CEO), Martin Schreiber (Chief Technology Officer), and members of the Company\'s finance and operations teams.

**Summary of Conclusions.** Based on our analysis, GW&Co. estimates that Arcadia has sustained total economic damages of **\$47,300,000**, as summarized in the table below and detailed in the sections that follow.

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Damages Category**                          **Amount**
  --------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Direct Damages (Contract Value Restitution)   \$14,700,000

  Lost Profits (5-Year Projection)              \$18,400,000

  Increased Operating Costs                     \$6,700,000

  Reputational Harm                             \$5,000,000

  DTPA Additional Damages                       \$2,500,000

  **Total Estimated Damages**                   **\$47,300,000**
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**[II. BACKGROUND AND FACTUAL BASIS]{.underline}**

**A. The MSLSA and Commercial Context**

Arcadia is a regional healthcare IT services provider operating electronic health record integration and analytics platforms for 23 hospitals and over 140 clinics across Texas and Louisiana, generating annual revenue of approximately \$62 million. In early 2022, Arcadia undertook a strategic initiative to replace its legacy analytics platform, HealthBridge 4.0, with Meridian\'s NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module. The transition was integral to Arcadia\'s growth strategy, which included expanding its service offerings to prospective hospital and clinic clients and enhancing its competitive position in the rapidly evolving healthcare IT marketplace.

On March 15, 2022, Arcadia entered into the MSLSA with Meridian for a total contract value of \$14,700,000, structured as follows:

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Contract Component**                      **Amount**
  ------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Software License Fees (3-Year Term)         \$6,200,000

  Implementation Services (Meridian Scope)    \$3,800,000

  Annual Maintenance and Support (Year 1)     \$1,900,000

  Training Services                           \$1,200,000

  Data Migration Consulting (Advisory Only)   \$1,600,000

  **Total MSLSA Value**                       **\$14,700,000**
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The contract contemplated a Go-Live Date of September 1, 2022. In the event, Go-Live did not occur until January 15, 2023 --- a delay of 137 days. During the extended implementation period, four Change Orders were executed, adding \$2,350,000 in scope. The total amount paid or payable by Arcadia under the MSLSA and Change Orders was therefore \$17,050,000.

**B. Deployment Failures and Post-Go-Live Deficiencies**

Following Go-Live, Arcadia experienced persistent and material deficiencies in NexusCore\'s performance, including intermittent system latency, report generation errors, data integrity issues, and incomplete API functionality. Arcadia logged 47 support tickets with Meridian between March and July 2023. Of particular significance, Arcadia\'s first written complaint --- an email from Dr. Okonkwo to Meridian\'s VP of Customer Success, Sandra Bledsoe, dated March 8, 2023 --- documented \"intermittent latency issues\" and \"occasional report generation errors\" that impaired Arcadia\'s ability to deliver reliable analytics to its hospital and clinic clients.

The cumulative effect of these failures was threefold: (i) Arcadia was unable to decommission HealthBridge 4.0 as planned, necessitating costly parallel operation of both platforms; (ii) Arcadia was required to hire additional IT staff and engage remediation consultants; and (iii) Arcadia suffered reputational damage in its market, culminating in the loss of two prospective hospital client relationships that were in advanced stages of negotiation.

**[III. DAMAGES ANALYSIS]{.underline}**

**A. Category 1: Direct Damages --- Contract Value Restitution (\$14,700,000)**

**Methodology.** GW&Co.\'s direct damages calculation is straightforward: Arcadia seeks full restitution of the total contract value paid under the MSLSA. The premise of this calculation is that Meridian fundamentally failed to deliver the software and services contemplated by the MSLSA, rendering the entire consideration paid by Arcadia a total loss.

**Calculation.**

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Payment Category**                      **Amount Paid**
  ----------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Software License Fees                     \$6,200,000

  Implementation Services                   \$3,800,000

  Annual Maintenance and Support (Year 1)   \$1,900,000

  Training Services                         \$1,200,000

  Data Migration Consulting (Advisory)      \$1,600,000

  **Total Direct Damages**                  **\$14,700,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Basis.** The direct damages figure represents the full value of the MSLSA exclusive of Change Order amounts. GW&Co. notes that the four Change Orders totaling \$2,350,000 were executed to address scope expansions and remediation work arising from the flawed implementation. While Arcadia paid a total of \$17,050,000 under the MSLSA and Change Orders combined, the direct damages claim is limited to the original \$14,700,000 contract value for purposes of this analysis. Arcadia reserves the right to amend its damages claim to include Change Order amounts to the extent supported by further discovery.

**Observations.** We note that Section 8.1 of the MSLSA contains a contractual limitation of liability capping each party\'s aggregate liability at the total fees paid or payable during the twelve-month period immediately preceding the event giving rise to liability. We further note that Section 8.2 contains a mutual waiver of consequential damages. The applicability and enforceability of these provisions is a legal question outside the scope of this financial analysis, but we flag their existence for counsel\'s consideration in presenting the damages claim. Our analysis quantifies the full economic harm sustained by Arcadia without applying contractual caps or limitations, which are addressed by counsel in the legal briefing.

**B. Category 2: Lost Profits (\$18,400,000)**

**Methodology.** GW&Co. calculated Arcadia\'s lost profits using a \"but-for\" analysis, comparing Arcadia\'s projected financial performance absent the NexusCore deployment failure against its actual and expected performance with the failed deployment. The lost profits calculation focuses on two prospective hospital client relationships that Arcadia was unable to consummate as a direct result of NexusCore\'s deficiencies.

**Factual Basis.** As of early 2023, Arcadia was in advanced negotiations with two prospective hospital clients (identified herein as \"Hospital A\" and \"Hospital B\") for multi-year analytics platform engagements. Both prospective clients conducted due diligence visits to Arcadia\'s facilities during Q1 2023 and observed the NexusCore deployment in its post-Go-Live state. According to Arcadia\'s management, both prospects expressed concerns regarding system performance and data reliability, and both ultimately elected not to proceed with Arcadia. The pipeline reports, correspondence, and internal meeting notes supporting these facts were provided to GW&Co. and have been produced to Arcadia\'s counsel.

**Projected Revenue from Lost Contracts.** Based on our review of Arcadia\'s pipeline documentation, comparable existing client contracts, and industry benchmarks for healthcare IT analytics engagements of similar scope:

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Metric**                        **Hospital A**    **Hospital B**    **Combined**
  --------------------------------- ----------------- ----------------- -----------------
  Estimated Annual Contract Value   \$2,180,000       \$1,500,000       \$3,680,000

  Contract Term (Expected)          5 Years           5 Years           5 Years

  Total Contract Revenue            \$10,900,000      \$7,500,000       \$18,400,000
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Calculation Detail.** The annual combined revenue from both prospective hospital contracts is estimated at \$3,680,000 per year. Over a five-year projection period, this yields total lost revenue of \$18,400,000.

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Year**                            **Projected Lost Revenue**
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Year 1 (2023)                       \$3,680,000

  Year 2 (2024)                       \$3,680,000

  Year 3 (2025)                       \$3,680,000

  Year 4 (2026)                       \$3,680,000

  Year 5 (2027)                       \$3,680,000

  **Total Lost Profits**              **\$18,400,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

**Key Assumptions and Limitations.**

> 1\. *Revenue projection.* The \$3,680,000 annual figure is based on the estimated contract values reflected in Arcadia\'s pipeline documentation and discussions with Arcadia\'s business development team. GW&Co. verified that these estimates are consistent with the pricing of Arcadia\'s existing comparable engagements with hospital clients of similar size and scope.
>
> 2\. *Projection period.* The five-year projection period is based on the standard initial contract term in Arcadia\'s client agreements and is consistent with industry norms for healthcare IT analytics engagements.
>
> 3\. *Gross revenue vs. net profit.* We note that the \$18,400,000 figure represents gross lost revenue from the two prospective contracts. We have not reduced this figure for associated costs of service delivery (estimated at approximately 35--40% of revenue based on Arcadia\'s historical margins), as Arcadia\'s counsel has indicated that the lost profits claim is presented on a gross revenue basis. If counsel determines that a net margin approach is more appropriate, the lost profits figure would be approximately \$10,300,000 to \$11,960,000 (applying a 60--65% gross margin).
>
> 4\. *Mitigation.* We have not applied any mitigation offset, as Arcadia has represented that it was unable to replace the lost Hospital A and Hospital B opportunities during the relevant period. This remains subject to further discovery.
>
> 5\. *Present value discount.* The above figures are presented on a nominal (undiscounted) basis. If a present-value discount is required, applying a discount rate of 8.5% (based on Arcadia\'s estimated weighted average cost of capital) would reduce the total to approximately \$15,200,000.

**C. Category 3: Increased Operating Costs (\$6,700,000)**

**Methodology.** GW&Co. calculated the incremental operating costs Arcadia incurred as a direct result of the NexusCore deployment failure by comparing Arcadia\'s actual operating expenditures against the budgeted expenditures under the scenario in which NexusCore had been successfully deployed on schedule.

**Component Breakdown.**

  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Cost Component**                             **Amount**        **Period**             **Description**
  ---------------------------------------------- ----------------- ---------------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Legacy System Maintenance (HealthBridge 4.0)   \$2,800,000       Mar 2022 -- Jul 2023   Costs to maintain and operate HealthBridge 4.0 beyond its planned decommission date, including hosting fees, vendor support, patch management, and internal administration

  Additional IT Staffing                         \$2,100,000       Jun 2022 -- Jul 2023   Salaries, benefits, and contractor fees for 8 additional IT personnel hired to support dual-platform operations and NexusCore troubleshooting

  Linden Park Remediation Fees                   \$1,800,000       Sep 2022 -- Jun 2023   Fees paid to Linden Park Consulting, LLC for data migration remediation, system integration rework, and ongoing technical support beyond original engagement scope

  **Total Increased Operating Costs**            **\$6,700,000**                          
  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Verification: \$2,800,000 + \$2,100,000 + \$1,800,000 = \$6,700,000**

**Supporting Detail --- Legacy System Maintenance (\$2,800,000).** Arcadia\'s original project plan contemplated full decommissioning of HealthBridge 4.0 by Q4 2022. As a result of the delayed and deficient NexusCore deployment, Arcadia was required to maintain HealthBridge 4.0 in parallel. The \$2,800,000 figure includes the following sub-components:

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Sub-Component**                             **Amount**
  --------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  HealthBridge 4.0 hosting and infrastructure   \$1,150,000

  Vendor support and maintenance fees           \$720,000

  Internal IT administration (allocated)        \$540,000

  Security patching and compliance updates      \$390,000

  **Total Legacy Maintenance**                  **\$2,800,000**
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Supporting Detail --- Additional IT Staffing (\$2,100,000).** The additional IT staffing costs comprise:

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Sub-Component**                                **FTEs / Contractors**   **Amount**
  ------------------------------------------------ ------------------------ -----------------------
  Full-time IT engineers (dual-platform support)   4 FTEs                   \$960,000

  Contract database administrators                 2 contractors            \$580,000

  NexusCore troubleshooting specialists            2 contractors            \$560,000

  **Total Additional IT Staffing**                 **8 personnel**          **\$2,100,000**
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Supporting Detail --- Linden Park Remediation Fees (\$1,800,000).** Arcadia retained Linden Park Consulting, LLC on July 1, 2022, as its third-party systems integrator for data migration activities. The original Linden Park engagement was budgeted at approximately \$1,200,000. However, due to complications arising during the NexusCore implementation --- including the need to remediate data migration scripts, rework system integrations, and provide extended on-site support --- Linden Park\'s total fees increased substantially. The \$1,800,000 figure represents the incremental costs above Arcadia\'s original budget for Linden Park\'s services. GW&Co. notes that Arcadia retained Linden Park independently and that the MSLSA designated data migration as Arcadia\'s responsibility. The allocation of these costs between Meridian\'s alleged failures and Arcadia\'s own implementation decisions is a factual and legal question beyond the scope of this financial analysis.

**D. Category 4: Reputational Harm (\$5,000,000)**

**Methodology.** Quantifying reputational harm is inherently more subjective than the other categories of damages analyzed herein. GW&Co. employed a multi-factor market positioning analysis to estimate the economic value of the reputational damage sustained by Arcadia as a result of the NexusCore deployment failure. Our approach considered the following factors:

> \(a\) *Market perception surveys.* GW&Co. engaged a third-party market research firm to conduct a targeted survey of 35 healthcare IT decision-makers in the Texas and Louisiana markets. The survey assessed perceptions of Arcadia\'s service reliability and technology capabilities before and after the NexusCore deployment period. Results indicated a measurable decline in Arcadia\'s perceived reliability among respondents with awareness of the NexusCore issues.
>
> \(b\) *Comparable precedent analysis.* GW&Co. reviewed published damages awards and settlements in comparable technology deployment disputes involving healthcare IT companies. The range of reputational harm awards in comparable cases ranged from approximately 5% to 15% of the plaintiff\'s annual revenue.
>
> \(c\) *Client retention risk assessment.* Arcadia\'s existing client base consists of 23 hospitals and over 140 clinics. GW&Co. modeled a scenario in which reputational damage results in elevated client attrition risk, estimating a potential loss of 2--4% of the existing revenue base over three years attributable to diminished market confidence.
>
> \(d\) *Brand equity valuation.* GW&Co. estimated the cost to Arcadia of rebuilding its market reputation through enhanced marketing, client engagement initiatives, and technology investment, using a cost-of-restoration approach.

**Estimate.** Based on the foregoing multi-factor analysis, GW&Co. estimates reputational harm damages of **\$5,000,000**. This figure represents approximately 8.1% of Arcadia\'s annual revenue of \$62 million, which falls within the range identified in our comparable precedent analysis.

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Analytical Factor**             **Estimated Range**          **Weighting**     **Contribution**
  --------------------------------- ---------------------------- ----------------- ------------------
  Market perception decline         \$3,000,000 -- \$6,000,000   30%               \$1,350,000

  Comparable precedent              \$3,100,000 -- \$9,300,000   25%               \$1,550,000

  Client attrition risk             \$1,200,000 -- \$4,800,000   25%               \$750,000

  Brand equity restoration          \$2,000,000 -- \$3,500,000   20%               \$550,000

  **Blended Estimate (Midpoint)**                                **100%**          **\$4,200,000**

  **Adjusted Estimate (Rounded)**                                                  **\$5,000,000**
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Limitations.** GW&Co. acknowledges that the reputational harm estimate is inherently more speculative than the other damages categories. The \$5,000,000 figure reflects our professional judgment based on available data and is presented as a reasonable approximation subject to refinement as additional information becomes available through discovery. The blended analytical midpoint of \$4,200,000 was adjusted upward to \$5,000,000 to account for qualitative factors not fully captured by the quantitative model, including the long-term strategic impact on Arcadia\'s ability to compete for large hospital system engagements.

**E. Category 5: DTPA Additional Damages (\$2,500,000)**

**Background.** Arcadia has asserted a claim under the Texas Deceptive Trade Practices --- Consumer Protection Act, Tex. Bus. & Com. Code Sections 17.41--17.63 (the \"DTPA\"), alleging that Meridian engaged in false, misleading, or deceptive acts or practices in connection with the marketing and sale of NexusCore. The DTPA provides for the recovery of actual damages and, in cases involving knowing or intentional conduct, additional damages of up to three times the amount of actual damages. See Tex. Bus. & Com. Code Section 17.50(b)(1).

**Calculation.** Arcadia\'s DTPA additional damages claim of \$2,500,000 is calculated as a component of the overall damages claim. We note that Arcadia\'s counsel has presented this figure as a partial additional damages claim, rather than a full treble damages calculation. Under Section 17.50(b)(1) of the DTPA, if the trier of fact finds that Meridian\'s conduct was committed knowingly, the court may award damages not to exceed three times the amount of actual damages in excess of \$1,000.

For reference, a full treble damages calculation on the direct damages component alone would yield:

  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Calculation**                              **Amount**
  -------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Direct Damages (Actual)                      \$14,700,000

  Treble Damages (3x)                          \$44,100,000

  Additional Damages Component (3x minus 1x)   \$29,400,000
  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Arcadia\'s claim of \$2,500,000 in DTPA additional damages represents a fraction of the full statutory entitlement. We understand from counsel that the \$2,500,000 figure reflects a conservative pleading strategy and that Arcadia reserves the right to seek enhanced DTPA damages based on evidence developed in discovery.

**Applicability Considerations.** GW&Co. notes the following considerations relevant to the DTPA claim:

> \(a\) *Exemption threshold.* Section 17.49(f) of the DTPA provides an exemption for transactions involving consideration exceeding \$500,000 if the consumer has assets of \$25 million or more, or is owned or controlled by an entity with assets of \$25 million or more. Arcadia\'s total assets as of its most recent audited financial statements are approximately \$41 million, which may place Arcadia above the DTPA asset threshold. However, the MSLSA consideration of \$14,700,000 clearly exceeds the \$500,000 transaction threshold. The interplay of these thresholds and their applicability to Arcadia\'s claim is a legal question addressed by counsel.
>
> \(b\) *Consumer status.* The DTPA\'s protections extend to \"consumers\" who seek or acquire goods or services by purchase or lease. Whether Arcadia qualifies as a \"consumer\" under the DTPA in the context of an enterprise software licensing transaction is a legal question beyond the scope of this financial analysis.

**Summary.**

  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **DTPA Damages Component**            **Amount**
  ------------------------------------- ------------------------------------------
  Additional Damages Claimed            \$2,500,000

  Full Treble Damages (for reference)   \$29,400,000 (additional component only)

  Arcadia\'s Claimed Amount             \$2,500,000 (conservative partial claim)
  --------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**[IV. SUMMARY OF TOTAL DAMAGES]{.underline}**

The following table summarizes the total estimated damages of \$47,300,000:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Category**                   **Amount**         **% of Total**    **Basis**
  ------------------------------ ------------------ ----------------- ---------------------------------------------
  Direct Damages (Restitution)   \$14,700,000       31.1%             Full MSLSA contract value

  Lost Profits                   \$18,400,000       38.9%             5-year projection: \$3,680,000/yr x 5 yrs

  Increased Operating Costs      \$6,700,000        14.2%             Legacy maintenance + IT staff + Linden Park

  Reputational Harm              \$5,000,000        10.6%             Multi-factor market positioning analysis

  DTPA Additional Damages        \$2,500,000        5.3%              Partial statutory additional damages

  **Total**                      **\$47,300,000**   **100.0%**        
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Proportional Analysis.** The \$47,300,000 total damages claim represents approximately:

> • 76.3% of Arcadia\'s annual revenue (\$62 million)
>
> • 3.21x the original MSLSA contract value (\$14,700,000)
>
> • 2.77x the total amounts paid or payable under the MSLSA and Change Orders (\$17,050,000)
>
> • 12.4% of Meridian\'s annual revenue (\$380 million)

**[V. KEY ASSUMPTIONS AND QUALIFICATIONS]{.underline}**

The damages analysis set forth herein is subject to the following assumptions, qualifications, and limitations:

1\. **Reliance on information provided.** GW&Co. has relied upon the accuracy and completeness of information provided by Arcadia, its management, and its counsel. We have not independently audited or verified Arcadia\'s financial statements or internal records, although we have performed limited analytical procedures to test the reasonableness of the data provided.

2\. **Legal determinations.** This analysis quantifies Arcadia\'s alleged economic damages from a financial perspective. It does not address legal questions regarding the enforceability of contractual limitations of liability, consequential damages waivers, choice-of-law provisions, or other legal defenses that may limit or bar recovery. Specifically, GW&Co. notes that Section 8.1 (Limitation of Liability), Section 8.2 (Consequential Damages Waiver), and Section 8.3 (Exclusive Remedies) of the MSLSA may, if enforceable, substantially limit the recoverable damages. The lost profits, increased operating costs, and reputational harm categories --- totaling \$30,100,000 of the \$47,300,000 claim --- may be characterized as consequential or indirect damages subject to the Section 8.2 waiver. These are legal questions for counsel and the Court.

3\. **Allocation of responsibility.** GW&Co.\'s analysis does not allocate responsibility for the NexusCore deployment failures as between Meridian, Arcadia, and Linden Park Consulting, LLC. The SOW-1 allocated data migration responsibilities to Arcadia, and Arcadia independently retained Linden Park as its systems integrator. Twelve of the 47 post-Go-Live support tickets related to data quality issues traceable to the Linden Park migration. The extent to which Arcadia\'s damages are attributable to its own acts or omissions, or to those of its third-party contractor, is a factual question for the trier of fact.

4\. **Acceptance testing.** GW&Co. notes that Arcadia did not deliver written notice of material nonconformity within the 30-day acceptance testing window following Go-Live (the window closed on February 14, 2023, and Arcadia\'s first written complaint was dated March 8, 2023, 22 days after the window closed). The legal effect of deemed acceptance under Section 5.3 of the MSLSA on Arcadia\'s damages claims is a question for counsel.

5\. **Mitigation of damages.** The analysis assumes that Arcadia took reasonable steps to mitigate its damages. GW&Co. has not independently assessed whether Arcadia\'s mitigation efforts were adequate or whether additional mitigation was available.

6\. **Lost profits assumptions.** The lost profits calculation is based on the assumption that Arcadia would have successfully consummated contracts with Hospital A and Hospital B \"but for\" the NexusCore deployment failure, and that such contracts would have been performed for the full five-year projection period. These assumptions are inherently uncertain and subject to challenge.

7\. **Reputational harm subjectivity.** The reputational harm estimate of \$5,000,000 is based on GW&Co.\'s professional judgment and a multi-factor analytical framework. Alternative methodologies could yield materially different results.

8\. **DTPA damages.** The DTPA additional damages figure of \$2,500,000 is presented as a conservative partial claim. GW&Co. expresses no opinion on whether Arcadia qualifies as a \"consumer\" under the DTPA or whether the Section 17.49(f) exemption applies.

9\. **Present value.** All figures are presented on a nominal (undiscounted) basis unless otherwise noted.

10\. **Revision rights.** GW&Co. reserves the right to supplement, revise, or update this analysis based on additional information obtained through discovery or other sources.

**[VI. CONCLUSION]{.underline}**

Based on the analysis described herein, GW&Co. estimates that Arcadia Health Systems, LLC has sustained total economic damages of **\$47,300,000** arising from the failed deployment of Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.\'s NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module. This estimate is subject to the assumptions, qualifications, and limitations set forth in Section V above.

The damages are summarized as follows:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Category**                        **Amount**
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Direct Damages (Restitution)        \$14,700,000

  Lost Profits (5-Year Projection)    \$18,400,000

  Increased Operating Costs           \$6,700,000

  Reputational Harm                   \$5,000,000

  DTPA Additional Damages             \$2,500,000

  **Total Estimated Damages**         **\$47,300,000**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

GW&Co. is prepared to present expert testimony in support of this analysis and to respond to any critiques or alternative analyses presented by Meridian\'s experts.

**GRAYSON WHITMORE & CO.**

GRAYSON WHITMORE & CO.

**WEXFORD HALE LLP** *Attorneys at Law*

300 West 6th Street, Suite 1500 Austin, Texas 78701

Telephone: (512) 555-0174 Facsimile: (512) 555-0175

July 25, 2023

VIA EMAIL AND FIRST CLASS MAIL

Margaret \"Meg\" Calloway, Esq. Stonebridge & Calloway LLP 600 Congress Avenue, Suite 2800 Austin, TX 78701

> Re: *Arcadia Health Systems, LLC v. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.* Response to Meridian\'s Position on DTPA Applicability Our File No.: WH-2023-0491

Dear Ms. Calloway:

We write in response to your letter of July 18, 2023, in which you assert on behalf of your client, Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. (\"Meridian\"), that the Texas Deceptive Trade Practices---Consumer Protection Act, Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §§ 17.41--17.63 (the \"DTPA\"), is inapplicable to the claims of our client, Arcadia Health Systems, LLC (\"Arcadia\"). Having carefully reviewed your arguments, we respectfully but firmly disagree. Arcadia intends to pursue its DTPA claims and is confident that such claims are well-founded in both fact and law.

**[I. Arcadia Qualifies as a \"Consumer\" Under the DTPA]{.underline}**

Your letter contends that Arcadia, as a business entity, cannot qualify as a \"consumer\" within the meaning of the DTPA. This argument fundamentally misapprehends the statute. Section 17.45(4) of the Texas Business and Commerce Code defines \"consumer\" as \"an individual, partnership, corporation, this state, or a subdivision or agency of this state who seeks or acquires by purchase or lease, any goods or services.\" The Texas Supreme Court has repeatedly held that business entities may qualify as consumers under the DTPA provided they sought or acquired the goods or services at issue. *See Amstadt v. U.S. Brass, Inc.*, 919 S.W.2d 644, 649 (Tex. 1996); *PPG Indus., Inc. v. JMB/Houston Ctrs. Partners Ltd. P\'ship*, 146 S.W.3d 79, 90 (Tex. 2004).

There is no dispute that Arcadia sought and acquired software licensing and implementation services from Meridian through the Master Software License and Services Agreement (\"MSLSA\") executed on March 15, 2022. Arcadia acquired these goods and services for use in its business operations --- specifically, to replace its legacy analytics platform, HealthBridge 4.0, with Meridian\'s NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module for deployment across the 23 hospitals and 140+ clinics that Arcadia serves across Texas and Louisiana. Arcadia paid or committed to pay \$14,700,000 under the MSLSA, plus an additional \$2,350,000 in Change Orders, for a total of \$17,050,000. Arcadia was, in every meaningful sense, a \"consumer\" of Meridian\'s goods and services.

Your suggestion that Arcadia\'s sophistication as a business entity somehow disqualifies it from consumer status under the DTPA is without support in the statute or the case law. The DTPA \"does not distinguish between sophisticated and unsophisticated consumers.\" *Melody Home Mfg. Co. v. Barnes*, 741 S.W.2d 349, 355 (Tex. 1987). The relevant inquiry is whether the claimant sought or acquired goods or services --- not whether the claimant is a large or small enterprise.

**[II. The Section 17.49(f) Exemption Does Not Apply]{.underline}**

The principal thrust of your letter is that Arcadia\'s DTPA claim is barred by the exemption set forth in Section 17.49(f) of the Business and Commerce Code, which provides that the DTPA does not apply to a \"cause of action arising from a transaction, a project, or a set of transactions relating to the same project, involving total consideration by the consumer of more than \$500,000\" where the consumer \"has assets of \$25 million or more.\" Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 17.49(f).

We acknowledge that the total consideration paid or payable under the MSLSA and associated Change Orders exceeds \$500,000. That element of the exemption is met. However, the exemption is conjunctive --- it requires *both* that the transaction exceed the \$500,000 consideration threshold *and* that the consumer possess assets of \$25 million or more. Arcadia does not meet the asset threshold.

As reflected in Arcadia\'s audited financial statements for the fiscal year ending December 31, 2022, Arcadia\'s total assets as of its most recent fiscal year-end were \$23.8 million. This figure is below the \$25 million statutory threshold. Accordingly, Section 17.49(f) does not exempt Meridian from DTPA liability in this matter.

We are prepared to produce Arcadia\'s audited financial statements in connection with any challenge Meridian may raise to this representation. We note that the burden of establishing the applicability of a DTPA exemption rests on the party asserting it --- in this case, Meridian. *See Cameron v. Terrell & Garrett, Inc.*, 618 S.W.2d 535, 541 (Tex. 1981) (exemptions from the DTPA are affirmative defenses to be proved by the party claiming them). Meridian cannot simply presume that Arcadia\'s total assets exceed \$25 million based on Arcadia\'s annual revenue or the size of the transaction at issue.

We further note that total assets under Section 17.49(f) is a defined accounting concept, referring to the aggregate book value of all assets reported on the entity\'s balance sheet. Arcadia\'s annual revenue of approximately \$62 million does not translate to total assets of \$25 million or more; Arcadia operates in a services-oriented sector with substantial operating expenses, and its balance sheet reflects its actual asset base rather than its top-line revenue. We trust this distinction is clear.

**[III. Arcadia\'s DTPA Claims Have Merit]{.underline}**

Beyond the threshold applicability questions, Arcadia\'s DTPA claims are meritorious. Meridian, through its Account Executive James Poletti and through its marketing materials and pre-sale presentations beginning October 12, 2021, made numerous representations regarding the capabilities, performance, and integration readiness of the NexusCore platform --- representations that proved materially false. These representations were made in Texas, relied upon in Texas, and caused injury in Texas. They constitute \"false, misleading, or deceptive acts or practices\" within the meaning of Section 17.46(b) and form the basis for Arcadia\'s claim for actual damages, treble damages, and attorneys\' fees under the DTPA.

We are confident that the evidentiary record, once fully developed, will demonstrate that Meridian\'s pre-sale representations were calculated to induce Arcadia to enter into a multimillion-dollar contract for software that Meridian knew, or should have known, would not perform as represented.

**[IV. Conclusion]{.underline}**

For the foregoing reasons, we respectfully reject Meridian\'s position that the DTPA is inapplicable to Arcadia\'s claims. We intend to assert a DTPA cause of action in Arcadia\'s forthcoming complaint. We remain open to discussing resolution of this matter on a global basis, but we will not forgo meritorious statutory claims based on Meridian\'s unpersuasive interpretation of the exemption provisions.

Please do not hesitate to contact me should you wish to discuss this matter further.

Very truly yours,

WEXFORD HALE LLP

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Jonathan Breckenridge Partner

Enclosures: None cc: Dr. Rachel Okonkwo, Managing Member / CEO, Arcadia Health Systems, LLC

This letter and any attachments are privileged and confidential and intended solely for the use of the individual or entity to whom they are addressed. If you have received this communication in error, please notify the sender immediately and destroy all copies.

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

{"file_path": "$WORKSPACE_DIR/build_docs.py", "content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Generate the three deliverables for the Arcadia v. Meridian MTD task."""
from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt, Inches, RGBColor
from docx.enum.text import WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH, WD_LINE_SPACING
from docx.enum.section import WD_SECTION
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement

FONT = "Times New Roman"
SIZE = 12

def new_doc():
    doc = Document()
    # default style
    st = doc.styles["Normal"]
    st.font.name = FONT
    st.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    st.element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn("w:eastAsia"), FONT)
    pf = st.paragraph_format
    pf.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.DOUBLE
    pf.space_after = Pt(0)
    pf.space_before = Pt(0)
    # margins
    for s in doc.sections:
        s.top_margin = Inches(1)
        s.bottom_margin = Inches(1)
        s.left_margin = Inches(1)
        s.right_margin = Inches(1)
    return doc

def add_page_numbers(doc):
    """Add 'Page X' centered at the bottom of every page."""
    for section in doc.sections:
        footer = section.footer
        footer.is_linked_to_previous = False
        p = footer.paragraphs[0]
        p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER
        p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        run = p.add_run()
        fld1 = OxmlElement("w:fldChar"); fld1.set(qn("w:fldCharType"), "begin")
        instr = OxmlElement("w:instrText"); instr.set(qn("xml:space"), "preserve"); instr.text = "PAGE"
        fld2 = OxmlElement("w:fldChar"); fld2.set(qn("w:fldCharType"), "end")
        run._r.append(fld1); run._r.append(instr); run._r.append(fld2)
        # prefix text
        p2 = footer.paragraphs[0]
        # build "Page " + field ; simpler: insert a run before
        pre = p2.insert_paragraph_before  # not used
    # Rebuild footer cleanly
    for section in doc.sections:
        footer = section.footer
        p = footer.paragraphs[0]
        # clear
        for r in list(p.runs):
            r._element.getparent().remove(r._element)
        p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER
        p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        run = p.add_run("Page ")
        run.font.name = FONT; run.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
        run2 = p.add_run()
        run2.font.name = FONT; run2.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
        fld1 = OxmlElement("w:fldChar"); fld1.set(qn("w:fldCharType"), "begin")
        instr = OxmlElement("w:instrText"); instr.set(qn("xml:space"), "preserve"); instr.text = "PAGE"
        fld2 = OxmlElement("w:fldChar"); fld2.set(qn("w:fldCharType"), "end")
        run2._r.append(fld1); run2._r.append(instr); run2._r.append(fld2)

def para(doc, text="", bold=False, italic=False, align=None, indent=None,
         spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.DOUBLE, space_after=0, size=SIZE, allcaps=False):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = spacing
    p.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(space_after)
    p.paragraph_format.space_before = Pt(0)
    if align is not None:
        p.alignment = align
    if indent is not None:
        p.paragraph_format.left_indent = Inches(indent)
    if text:
        r = p.add_run(text)
        r.bold = bold; r.italic = italic
        r.font.name = FONT; r.font.size = Pt(size)
        if allcaps:
            r.font.all_caps = True
    return p

def runs(p, segments):
    """segments: list of (text, bold, italic)"""
    for seg in segments:
        text, bold, italic = (seg + (False, False))[:3] if isinstance(seg, tuple) else (seg, False, False)
        r = p.add_run(text)
        r.bold = bold; r.italic = italic
        r.font.name = FONT; r.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    return p

def blockquote(doc, text):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
    p.paragraph_format.left_indent = Inches(0.5)
    p.paragraph_format.right_indent = Inches(0.5)
    p.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
    p.paragraph_format.space_before = Pt(0)
    r = p.add_run(text)
    r.font.name = FONT; r.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    return p

def heading(doc, text, level=1):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.DOUBLE
    p.paragraph_format.space_before = Pt(6)
    p.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
    p.paragraph_format.keep_with_next = True
    r = p.add_run(text)
    r.bold = True
    r.font.name = FONT; r.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    if level == 1:
        r.font.all_caps = True
    return p

def caption(doc, title_lines, title_bold_lines):
    """Build the standard federal court caption."""
    # Court name lines, centered, bold
    for line in ["UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT", "WESTERN DISTRICT OF TEXAS", "AUSTIN DIVISION"]:
        p = doc.add_paragraph()
        p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER
        p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        p.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
        r = p.add_run(line); r.bold = True; r.font.name = FONT; r.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    # blank
    para(doc, "")
    # Two-column-ish caption using a table for alignment
    tbl = doc.add_table(rows=5, cols=2)
    tbl.autofit = True
    cells = [
        ("ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC,", "Case No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA"),
        ("Plaintiff,", ""),
        ("v.", "The Honorable Catherine M. Alvarez"),
        ("MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.,", "United States District Judge"),
        ("Defendant.", ""),
    ]
    for i, (left, right) in enumerate(cells):
        lc = tbl.cell(i, 0); rc = tbl.cell(i, 1)
        lp = lc.paragraphs[0]; lp.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        lp.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
        lr = lp.add_run(left); lr.font.name = FONT; lr.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
        rp = rc.paragraphs[0]; rp.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        rp.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
        rp.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.RIGHT
        rr = rp.add_run(right); rr.font.name = FONT; rr.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    # remove table borders
    set_no_borders(tbl)
    para(doc, "")
    # Title
    for i, line in enumerate(title_lines):
        p = doc.add_paragraph()
        p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER
        p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        p.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
        r = p.add_run(line); r.bold = title_bold_lines[i]; r.font.name = FONT; r.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    para(doc, "")

def set_no_borders(tbl):
    tblPr = tbl._tbl.tblPr
    borders = OxmlElement("w:tblBorders")
    for edge in ("top","left","bottom","right","insideH","insideV"):
        e = OxmlElement(f"w:{edge}")
        e.set(qn("w:val"), "nil")
        borders.append(e)
    tblPr.append(borders)

def hrule(doc):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
    p.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
    pPr = p._p.get_or_add_pPr()
    pbdr = OxmlElement("w:pBdr")
    bottom = OxmlElement("w:bottom")
    bottom.set(qn("w:val"), "single"); bottom.set(qn("w:sz"), "6")
    bottom.set(qn("w:space"), "1"); bottom.set(qn("w:color"), "auto")
    pbdr.append(bottom); pPr.append(pbdr)

def sig_block(doc, lines):
    for line in lines:
        p = doc.add_paragraph()
        p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        p.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
        r = p.add_run(line); r.font.name = FONT; r.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
        if line.startswith("STONEBRIDGE") or line.startswith("By:") or line.startswith("Margaret") or line.startswith("David"):
            pass

print("helpers loaded")"}

    # blank
    para(doc, "")
    # Two-column-ish caption using a table for alignment
    tbl = doc.add_table(rows=5, cols=2)
    tbl.autofit = True
    cells = [
        ("ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC,", "Case No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA"),
        ("Plaintiff,", ""),
        ("v.", "The Honorable Catherine M. Alvarez"),
        ("MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.,", "United States District Judge"),
        ("Defendant.", ""),
    ]
    for i, (left, right) in enumerate(cells):
        lc = tbl.cell(i, 0); rc = tbl.cell(i, 1)
        lp = lc.paragraphs[0]; lp.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        lp.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
        lr = lp.add_run(left); lr.font.name = FONT; lr.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
        rp = rc.paragraphs[0]; rp.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        rp.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
        rp.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.RIGHT
        rr = rp.add_run(right); rr.font.name = FONT; rr.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    # remove table borders
    set_no_borders(tbl)
    para(doc, "")
    # Title
    for i, line in enumerate(title_lines):
        p = doc.add_paragraph()
        p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER
        p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        p.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
        r = p.add_run(line); r.bold = title_bold_lines[i]; r.font.name = FONT; r.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    para(doc, "")

def set_no_borders(tbl):
    tblPr = tbl._tbl.tblPr
    borders = OxmlElement("w:tblBorders")
    for edge in ("top","left","bottom","right","insideH","insideV"):
        e = OxmlElement(f"w:{edge}")
        e.set(qn("w:val"), "nil")
        borders.append(e)
    tblPr.append(borders)

def hrule(doc):
    p = doc.add_paragraph()
    p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
    p.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
    pPr = p._p.get_or_add_pPr()
    pbdr = OxmlElement("w:pBdr")
    bottom = OxmlElement("w:bottom")
    bottom.set(qn("w:val"), "single"); bottom.set(qn("w:sz"), "6")
    bottom.set(qn("w:space"), "1"); bottom.set(qn("w:color"), "auto")
    pbdr.append(bottom); pPr.append(pbdr)

def sig_block(doc, lines):
    for line in lines:
        p = doc.add_paragraph()
        p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
        p.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(0)
        r = p.add_run(line); r.font.name = FONT; r.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
        if line.startswith("STONEBRIDGE") or line.startswith("By:") or line.startswith("Margaret") or line.startswith("David"):
            pass

print("helpers loaded")"}
