    para(doc, "It is further ORDERED that, in the alternative and only in the event the Court's dismissal of Count I is reversed or modified on appeal, Arcadia's recoverable damages on Count I shall be limited to the exclusive remedies and the limitation of liability set forth in the Master Software License and Services Agreement, including the aggregate-liability cap in Section 8.1 and the consequential-damages waiver in Section 8.2, and that all claims for lost profits, increased operating costs, reputational harm, punitive damages, treble damages, and attorneys' fees are barred.")
    para(doc, "It is further ORDERED that Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC shall take nothing by its First Amended Complaint, that all claims against Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. are dismissed with prejudice, and that Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. is entitled to its reasonable and necessary attorneys' fees and costs to the extent permitted by applicable law and the terms of the Master Software License and Services Agreement.")

# ============================================================
# DOCUMENT 3: COVER MEMO (internal, threshold issues)
# ============================================================
def build_memo():
    doc = new_doc()
    add_page_numbers(doc)
    # Header
    p = doc.add_paragraph(); p.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER
    p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
    r = p.add_run("STONEBRIDGE & CALLOWAY LLP"); r.bold = True; r.font.name = FONT; r.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    p2 = doc.add_paragraph(); p2.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER
    p2.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
    r2 = p2.add_run("600 Congress Avenue, Suite 2800  \u2022  Austin, Texas 78701"); r2.font.name = FONT; r2.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    para(doc, "")
    p3 = doc.add_paragraph(); p3.alignment = WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER
    p3.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE
    r3 = p3.add_run("PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL  \u2014  ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT"); r3.bold = True; r3.font.name = FONT; r3.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    para(doc, "")
    heading(doc, "MEMORANDUM")
    # To/From/Date/Re block
    for label, val in [
        ("TO:", "Margaret \u201cMeg\u201d Calloway, Lead Partner; David Arsenault, Senior Associate"),
        ("FROM:", "Litigation Team, Stonebridge & Calloway LLP"),
        ("DATE:", "January 12, 2024"),
        ("RE:", "Threshold and Strategic Issues \u2014 Defendant's Rule 12(b)(6) Motion to Dismiss \u2014 Arcadia Health Systems, LLC v. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc., No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA (W.D. Tex., Austin Div.)"),
    ]:
        p = doc.add_paragraph(); p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule = WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE; p.paragraph_format.space_after = Pt(2)
        r = p.add_run(label + "  "); r.bold = True; r.font.name = FONT; r.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
        r2 = p.add_run(val); r2.font.name = FONT; r2.font.size = Pt(SIZE)
    hrule(doc)
    para(doc, "")

    para(doc, "This memorandum flags the threshold and strategic issues the team should resolve before and shortly after filing the attached motion to dismiss, proposed order, and this cover memo. The issues are listed in approximate order of urgency. The motion itself is drafted to move to dismiss all five counts; the points below concern matters that either sit outside the four corners of the brief, that may affect how or whether the brief is filed, or that require follow-up.")

    heading(doc, "1. Subject-Matter Jurisdiction \u2014 A Likely Complete-Diversity Defect (HIGHEST PRIORITY)")
    para(doc, "Our own Notice of Removal alleged that Arcadia is a citizen of Texas only, based solely on Dr. Okonkwo's domicile. That analysis is almost certainly wrong and may be fatal to the Court's subject-matter jurisdiction. An LLC's citizenship is determined by the citizenship of all of its members. Harvey v. Grey Wolf Drilling Co., 542 F.3d 1077, 1080 (5th Cir. 2008). Arcadia's operating agreement (produced as Exhibit 7) and the FAC itself (\u00b6\u00b6 7\u20138) disclose three members: Dr. Okonkwo (Texas), Martin Schreiber (Texas), and Apex Medical Ventures, LP (a Delaware limited partnership). A limited partnership's citizenship is traced to all of its partners. Carden v. Arkoma Assocs., 494 U.S. 185, 195\u201396 (1990). Apex's general partner is Apex Medical Ventures GP, Inc., a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business in Wilmington, Delaware\u2014a citizen of Delaware. Apex's limited partner is the Okonkwo Family Trust (beneficiary domiciled in Texas).")
    para(doc, "Accordingly, Arcadia is a citizen of both Texas and Delaware (through Apex GP, Inc.). Meridian is a citizen of both Delaware (incorporation) and Texas (principal place of business, Austin). Because both parties share Delaware and Texas citizenship, complete diversity does not exist, and the Court likely lacks subject-matter jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. \u00a7 1332(a). Subject-matter jurisdiction cannot be waived and may be raised by the Court sua sponte at any time. Arbaugh v. Y & H Corp., 546 U.S. 500, 514 (2006).")
    para(doc, "Strategic implications. This is awkward because Meridian removed the case and would be moving to remand on a defect it created. Options to discuss with the client: (a) file the 12(b)(6) motion on the merits and let the jurisdictional issue be raised sua sponte or by Arcadia (risk: the Court may remand and the motion becomes moot, but the substantive defenses travel with the case to state court); (b) move to remand ourselves (cleanest legally, but concedes our removal was improper and forfeits the federal forum and Judge Alvarez's rigorous pleading standards); (c) file the 12(b)(6) motion and concurrently flag the jurisdictional issue to the Court. We should confirm the citizenship analysis against the Apex partnership agreement (still outstanding) and decide before the January 15 deadline. Note: the FAC's own allegations (\u00b6\u00b6 7\u20138) disclose the membership structure that destroys diversity, so the defect is apparent on the face of the operative pleading.")

    heading(doc, "2. Post-Answer Motion \u2014 Rule 12(b)(6) vs. Rule 12(c)")
    para(doc, "There is a direct conflict between the Court's Standing Order and the Scheduling Order. Standing Order \u00a7 3.4 states that a post-answer motion preserved in the Answer \u201cshall [be] treat[ed]\u2026 as a motion to dismiss, not as a motion for judgment on the pleadings under Rule 12(c).\u201d The Scheduling Order states the opposite: \u201cThe Court will treat any such motion as a Rule 12(c) motion for judgment on the pleadings, evaluated under the same standard as a Rule 12(b)(6) motion.\u201d The Answer preserved the defense (First Affirmative Defense; Part III reservation; letter to the Court dated November 8, 2023), so preservation is not in dispute. The practical effect is minimal because the standard is identical (Twombly/Iqbal), and on a 12(c) motion the Court may also consider the Answer. The motion is captioned as a 12(b)(6) motion per the task and the Standing Order; we should be prepared for the Court to treat it as 12(c) and should not object.")

    heading(doc, "3. Choice of Law \u2014 The FAC Misstates the Governing Law for Count I")
    para(doc, "Section 12.7 of the MSLSA selects Delaware law, without regard to conflict-of-law principles, to govern \u201cthis Agreement.\u201d The FAC nevertheless pleads that \u201cTexas law governs this breach of contract claim\u201d (FAC \u00b6 76) and \u201cthis fraud claim\u201d (\u00b6 89), \u201cthis negligent misrepresentation claim\u201d (\u00b6 97), and \u201cthis unjust enrichment claim\u201d (\u00b6 104). The motion argues Delaware law governs Count I and that the tort claims fail under either Delaware or Texas law. We should be prepared to brief choice of law more fully if Arcadia contests it. The DTPA (Count V) is a Texas statute regardless of the choice-of-law clause. For unjust enrichment (Count IV), both Delaware and Texas bar the claim where an express contract governs, so the choice of law is immaterial to the result.")

    heading(doc, "4. DTPA \u2014 The Section 17.49(f) Asset Threshold and a Contradiction in Arcadia's Own Submissions")
    para(doc, "The \u00a7 17.49(f) exemption is our cleanest DTPA ground, but it has a wrinkle. Arcadia's own July 25, 2023 letter confirms that the current statute is conjunctive: it requires both (a) total consideration exceeding $500,000 and (b) the consumer having assets of $25 million or more. The $500,000 element is indisputably met ($17.05 million). The $25 million asset element is where the record is messy:")
    para(doc, "  \u2022 Arcadia's counsel represented in the July 25, 2023 letter that Arcadia's total assets were \u201c$23.8 million\u201d\u2014below the threshold.", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    para(doc, "  \u2022 Arcadia's own damages expert (Grayson Whitmore, July 28, 2023) reported Arcadia's total assets at \u201capproximately $41 million\u201d\u2014above the threshold.", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    para(doc, "  \u2022 The FAC alleges \u201csubstantial infrastructure investments exceeding $30 million\u201d (FAC \u00b6\u00b6 38, 117), which supports (though does not conclusively establish) assets above $25 million.", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    para(doc, "This direct contradiction in Arcadia's own submissions is both a litigation asset and a complication. On a 12(b)(6) motion, the Court may be reluctant to find the exemption established if Arcadia's asset size is not conclusively shown on the face of the complaint. The motion therefore argues the exemption is satisfied (relying on the FAC's $30 million investment allegation and the $41 million figure in Arcadia's own damages report) and, in the alternative, presses the puffery, disclaimer, and producing-cause grounds so that the DTPA count fails even if the asset threshold is not resolved at the pleading stage. We should obtain Arcadia's audited financial statements (counsel for Arcadia offered to produce them) and be prepared to renew the \u00a7 17.49(f) argument on summary judgment if needed. The burden of proving the exemption is on Meridian. Cameron v. Terrell & Garrett, Inc., 618 S.W.2d 535, 541 (Tex. 1981).")

    heading(doc, "5. DTPA \u2014 Verify the Current Text of Section 17.49(f)")
    para(doc, "The record contains inconsistent statements of the statute. Our research memo states the exemption as \u201c$500,000 + consumer not an individual.\u201d Arcadia's letter, our Answer (Thirteenth Affirmative Defense), and our pre-suit response letter state it as \u201c$500,000 + $25 million in assets.\u201d These are not the same test. We must verify the current statutory text (and any 2011/2019 amendments) before the brief is finalized and align the motion, the Answer, and our pre-suit correspondence. The motion currently tracks the conjunctive $500,000 + $25 million-assets formulation as stated by Arcadia's own counsel.")

    heading(doc, "6. DTPA \u2014 Possible Failure to Satisfy the 60-Day Pre-Suit Notice (Section 17.505(a))")
    para(doc, "Section 17.505(a) requires a consumer to give written notice at least 60 days before filing suit. Arcadia's DTPA demand letter is dated June 15, 2023; the original petition was filed August 7, 2023\u2014only 53 days later. (There is also a date discrepancy: our July 10, 2023 response refers to Arcadia's letter as dated \u201cJune 26, 2023,\u201d which would make the gap only 42 days.) Either way, suit appears to have been filed before the 60-day period elapsed. This is a procedural defect more naturally raised by a motion to abate or in the answer than by a 12(b)(6) motion, and courts often treat it as curable rather than jurisdictional. We have not made it a primary ground in the motion, but it should be raised separately (or held in reserve) and the demand-letter date reconciled.")

    heading(doc, "7. Jury-Trial Waiver (Section 12.9)")
    para(doc, "The MSLSA contains a mutual jury-trial waiver, which Arcadia's counsel expressly accepted during negotiations. The Scheduling Order recites that \u201c[t]he parties have waived their right to a jury trial pursuant to Section 12.9\u201d and sets a bench trial. The FAC nonetheless demands a jury (FAC \u00b6 122), and our Answer conditionally demands a jury \u201cto the extent that the Court determines the jury trial waiver\u2026 is not enforceable.\u201d Watch for an Arcadia challenge to the waiver on the theory that fraudulent inducement voids the waiver or that the waiver does not extend to the fraud/DTPA claims. This is a trial-phase issue, not a motion-to-dismiss issue, but it should be tracked.")

    heading(doc, "8. Documents the Court May Consider on the Motion")
    para(doc, "The motion relies on documents beyond the FAC. Under Lormand, the Court may consider documents referenced in and central to the claims without converting the motion to summary judgment. Safest: the MSLSA, SOW-1, and the four Change Orders (all referenced in the FAC and central to every count). Also strong: the written proposal (Exhibit H), the sales presentation (Exhibit G), Dr. Okonkwo's March 8, 2023 email (Exhibit E), and the Grayson Whitmore damages analysis (Exhibit F)\u2014all listed in the FAC's exhibit list. Pushing the boundary: the Schreiber internal memo and the August 25, 2022 project status report, which are referenced in the FAC (\u00b6\u00b6 31, 44) and central to reliance and causation but were not attached. Be prepared for Arcadia to argue that consideration of the project-status report and Schreiber memo converts the motion to summary judgment; we should be ready to respond that both are referenced in and central to the claims, or to rely primarily on the contract documents and the FAC's own allegations.")

    heading(doc, "9. Procedural Compliance with the Standing Order")
    para(doc, "  \u2022 Certificate of conference. Standing Order \u00a7 3.1 requires a certificate of conference for dispositive motions, filed as a separate document. The motion includes a certificate of conference; we should also file it as a standalone document to be safe, and ensure the meet-and-confer with Breckenridge actually occurs and is documented before filing.", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    para(doc, "  \u2022 Page limit. The motion is within the 25-page limit (Standing Order \u00a7 3.2), exclusive of the caption, signature block, and certificates.", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    para(doc, "  \u2022 Reply page limit conflict. The Standing Order allows 15-page replies; the Scheduling Order says 10-page replies. We should plan for 10 pages to be safe.", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    para(doc, "  \u2022 Proposed order. Standing Order \u00a7 3.5 requires the proposed order as a separate .docx filing (not embedded in the motion). The attached proposed order is drafted accordingly; update the docket entry number within one business day of filing.", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    para(doc, "  \u2022 Deadline. The dispositive-motion deadline is January 15, 2024 (Scheduling Order \u00a7 III.A).", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)

    heading(doc, "10. Strategy on Count I \u2014 Full Dismissal vs. Damages Limitation")
    para(doc, "The motion seeks full dismissal of Count I but, in the alternative, asks that damages be limited to the contractual cap and exclusive remedies. Full dismissal of a breach-of-contract claim at the pleading stage is aggressive, and Judge Alvarez may be reluctant to dismiss it entirely where the FAC alleges non-delivery of conforming software. The deemed-acceptance and warranty-expiration arguments are strong, but the safer play may be to press the damages-limitation alternative heavily. The proposed order grants full dismissal but, in the alternative, limits damages\u2014matching the motion's primary/alternative structure. We should be prepared for the Court to deny full dismissal of Count I but grant the damages limitation, which would still eliminate roughly $30 million of the $47.3 million demand.")

    heading(doc, "11. The \u201cSeamless Integration\u201d Representation \u2014 Evidentiary Note")
    para(doc, "The FAC alleges Poletti said NexusCore \u201cwill integrate seamlessly with any EHR platform\u201d (FAC \u00b6 25). Our Answer avers that Poletti's contemporaneous notes reflect that he actually said integration is \u201csubject to proper configuration.\u201d The contemporaneous notes are extrinsic and should not be the primary basis at the pleading stage. Instead, the motion relies on the written proposal (Exhibit H), which expressly disclaims \u201cseamless\u201d integration, and the sales presentation (Exhibit G), which qualifies integration as \u201csubject to proper configuration.\u201d Both are referenced in the FAC and central to the claims. Preserve the Poletti notes for summary judgment or trial.")

    heading(doc, "12. Outstanding Factual Items")
    para(doc, "  \u2022 Confirm the citizenship analysis with the Apex Medical Ventures, LP partnership agreement (still outstanding).", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    para(doc, "  \u2022 Obtain Arcadia's audited financial statements to nail down the \u00a7 17.49(f) asset threshold (Arcadia's counsel offered to produce them).", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    para(doc, "  \u2022 Reconcile the DTPA demand-letter date (June 15 vs. June 26, 2023) for the \u00a7 17.505(a) 60-day-notice analysis.", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    para(doc, "  \u2022 Obtain Linden Park's scope-of-work and correspondence to strengthen the causation/data-migration arguments.", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    para(doc, "  \u2022 Calculate the Section 8.1 cap amount (fees paid or payable in the 12 months preceding the \u201cevent giving rise to liability\u201d) from Meridian's billing records; the \u201cevent\u201d date is ambiguous and affects the cap.", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)

    para(doc, "")
    para(doc, "Please advise on the jurisdictional question (Item 1) at your earliest convenience, as it may affect whether and how we file on January 15.", spacing=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)

    out = "output/cover-memo.docx"
    doc.save(out)
    print("saved", out)
    return doc

if __name__ == "__main__":
    build_motion()
    build_order()
    build_memo()
    print("ALL DONE")

result:
**UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT**

**WESTERN DISTRICT OF TEXAS**

**AUSTIN DIVISION**

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------
  ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC,        Case No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA
  ----------------------------------- ------------------------------------
  Plaintiff,                          

  v\.                                 The Honorable Catherine M. Alvarez

  MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.,     United States District Judge

  Defendant.                          
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------

**DEFENDANT MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.\'S MOTION TO DISMISS**

**PLAINTIFF\'S FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT PURSUANT TO**

**FEDERAL RULE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE 12(b)(6)**

**TO THE HONORABLE COURT:**

Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. ("Meridian" or "Defendant"), by and through its undersigned counsel, Stonebridge & Calloway LLP, pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(6) and the Court\'s Standing Order on Motion Practice (§ 3.4), files this Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC\'s ("Arcadia" or "Plaintiff") First Amended Complaint (Dkt. No. 12) (the "FAC") and all five counts asserted therein, and in support thereof would show the Court as follows:

**I. INTRODUCTION**

This is a breach-of-contract dispute dressed up as a tort and statutory case. Arcadia is a sophisticated, \$62-million-revenue healthcare IT company that was represented by experienced outside counsel (Wexford Hale LLP) in negotiating a \$14.7 million Master Software License and Services Agreement (the "MSLSA") containing a comprehensive, heavily negotiated allocation of risk. Having received the licensed software, accepted it by operation of the contract\'s deemed-acceptance provision, used it for months, and executed four Change Orders during implementation, Arcadia now demands \$47.3 million---more than three times the contract value---by recasting an ordinary commercial disagreement as fraud, negligent misrepresentation, unjust enrichment, and a violation of the Texas Deceptive Trade Practices--Consumer Protection Act (the "DTPA").

Each of the five counts fails as a matter of law. The DTPA claim (Count V) is barred by a statutory exemption for large commercial transactions. The unjust enrichment claim (Count IV) is unavailable because an express, valid contract governs the same subject matter. The fraud claim (Count II) fails the particularity requirements of Rule 9(b), the economic loss doctrine, the puffery doctrine, and the MSLSA\'s integration clause. The negligent misrepresentation claim (Count III) fails for lack of an independent duty and because Arcadia\'s damages are contractual, not out-of-pocket. And the breach of contract claim (Count I) is defeated by deemed acceptance, the expired limited warranty, the contract\'s exclusive-remedy provisions, and the MSLSA\'s express allocation of data-migration responsibility to Arcadia. In the alternative, even if Count I survives, Arcadia\'s damages are contractually capped and its consequential damages are waived.

Meridian preserved its Rule 12(b)(6) defenses in its Answer (Dkt. No. 14), including by asserting a First Affirmative Defense for failure to state a claim and a separate reservation of its right to file a motion to dismiss, consistent with the Court\'s Standing Order. To the extent the Court treats this post-answer motion as a motion for judgment on the pleadings under Rule 12(c) pursuant to the Scheduling Order (Dkt. No. \_\_), the governing legal standard is identical, and the motion should be granted for the reasons set forth below.

**II. STATEMENT OF FACTS**

A. The parties and the MSLSA. Meridian is a Delaware corporation that licenses its NexusCore enterprise software platform. Arcadia is a Texas limited liability company with approximately \$62 million in annual revenue that operates analytics platforms for 23 hospitals and over 140 clinics. On March 15, 2022, the parties executed the MSLSA and Statement of Work #1 ("SOW-1") for deployment of the NexusCore Healthcare Analytics Module, for a total contract value of \$14.7 million. FAC ¶¶ 6--9, 33--35. The MSLSA is governed by Delaware law (Section 12.7) and contains an exclusive forum-selection clause designating the federal and state courts in Austin, Travis County, Texas (Section 12.8).

B. The MSLSA\'s risk-allocation provisions. The MSLSA is a comprehensive, negotiated agreement that allocates risk between two sophisticated commercial parties. Section 8.1 caps each party\'s aggregate liability at the total fees paid or payable during the twelve-month period preceding the event giving rise to liability. Section 8.2 excludes all indirect, incidental, special, consequential, punitive, and exemplary damages, including lost profits, goodwill, and reputational harm. Section 8.3 designates the contract\'s remedies as exclusive. Section 9.1 provides a limited 90-day warranty (running from Go-Live) with repair, replacement, or pro-rata refund as the sole remedy. Section 9.4 disclaims all other warranties, express and implied. Section 5.3 provides a 30-day acceptance-testing window with deemed acceptance if the licensee fails to deliver written notice of material nonconformity. Section 12.1 is a comprehensive integration clause superseding all prior representations. Section 12.9 contains a mutual jury-trial waiver.

C. Arcadia\'s sophistication and counsel. Arcadia was represented by Wexford Hale LLP throughout negotiations. The negotiation record confirms that Arcadia\'s counsel specifically negotiated the limitation of liability, the warranty, the acceptance-testing procedures, the forum-selection clause, and the integration clause. Arcadia accepted the consequential-damages waiver "as drafted"---"knowingly and with the benefit of legal counsel"---and expressly withdrew its request for a fraud carve-out to the integration clause, representing that Arcadia was "comfortable proceeding on the basis of the express contractual terms rather than pre-contractual representations." Arcadia\'s counsel further memorialized that Arcadia was "entering this agreement with eyes wide open regarding the risk allocation framework."

D. Arcadia\'s independent due diligence. Before contracting, Arcadia\'s CTO, Martin Schreiber, conducted extensive due diligence, including reference calls with two existing Meridian healthcare clients and a two-week proof-of-concept trial. Schreiber\'s January 19, 2022 internal memorandum concluded that "\[i\]ntegration complexity is manageable but will require a skilled SI partner," that the standard API connectors "do not natively support several legacy data exchange formats," and that Arcadia should "\[e\]ngage outside counsel for contract review" with "particular attention... to limitation of liability provisions, warranty terms, \[and\] acceptance testing procedures." He strongly recommended retaining an independent systems integrator for data migration. FAC ¶ 31.

E. The written proposal and sales presentation disclaimers. Meridian\'s December 9, 2021 written proposal---delivered before the MSLSA and referenced in the FAC (Exhibit H)---expressly disclaimed that "\[e\]stimated timelines and performance metrics are provided for planning purposes only and do not constitute guarantees," that "Meridian does not warrant that integration with any particular EHR system will be seamless, error-free, or achievable within any specific timeframe," and that Arcadia "should not rely solely on the information in this proposal." The October 12, 2021 sales presentation (Exhibit G) likewise stated that NexusCore "is designed to integrate with major EHR platforms through our standard API framework, subject to proper configuration," and included a disclaimer that the presentation "does not constitute a warranty or guarantee of performance."

F. Data migration was Arcadia\'s responsibility. SOW-1 Section 3.2 and Exhibit C expressly provide that Arcadia "shall be solely responsible for all data migration activities," including extraction, transformation, and loading, and that Meridian\'s role was limited to advisory consulting with "no responsibility for the accuracy, completeness, or integrity of migrated data." Arcadia independently retained Linden Park Consulting, LLC ("Linden Park") to perform the migration.

G. The 137-day Go-Live delay was caused by Arcadia and Linden Park. Meridian\'s August 25, 2022 project status report (FAC ¶ 44; Exhibit D) identified three root causes for the delay beyond the September 1, 2022 target: (1) Arcadia\'s 45-day gap in designating a replacement Project Manager after Kevin Liu\'s departure on April 28, 2022; (2) Arcadia\'s 75-day-late delivery of complete API specifications (due May 6, 2022; delivered July 20, 2022); and (3) critical errors in Linden Park\'s data-migration scripts. Go-Live occurred on January 15, 2023. FAC ¶ 45.

H. Deemed acceptance and the expired warranty. Under Section 5.3, Arcadia had 30 days following Go-Live to deliver written notice of material nonconformity. The acceptance window closed on February 14, 2023. Arcadia\'s first written complaint---Dr. Okonkwo\'s March 8, 2023 email (FAC ¶ 50; Exhibit E)---was sent 22 days after the window closed and referenced only "intermittent latency issues" and "occasional report generation errors." Acceptance was therefore deemed granted. The Section 9.1 limited warranty expired 90 days later, on April 15, 2023.

I. The support-ticket record. Between March and July 2023, Arcadia submitted 47 support tickets. Thirty-one were resolved within SLA timeframes; twelve were traceable to data-quality errors caused by Linden Park\'s migration; and only four involved NexusCore software defects, all of which Meridian patched on May 2 and June 19, 2023. All 47 tickets were resolved by July 31, 2023, and ticket volume declined steadily---a normal post-deployment stabilization pattern. FAC ¶¶ 51--54.

J. Change Order CO-004. On December 5, 2022, the parties executed Change Order CO-004 (\$900,000) for data-remediation services to correct errors in Linden Park\'s migration scripts. CO-004 expressly acknowledges that "the data integrity issues... arose from errors in data migration scripts prepared and executed by Linden Park Consulting, LLC, and not from any defect in the NexusCore software or any act or omission of \[Meridian\]." FAC ¶ 47.

K. The claimed damages. Arcadia seeks \$47.3 million: \$14.7 million in "direct damages/restitution," \$18.4 million in lost profits (a five-year projection based on two unnamed prospective clients), \$6.7 million in increased operating costs (including \$1.8 million in Linden Park remediation fees), \$5 million in reputational harm, and \$2.5 million in DTPA additional damages. FAC ¶¶ 61--67. Over \$30 million of this demand consists of consequential damages expressly waived by Section 8.2.

**III. LEGAL STANDARD**

A Rule 12(b)(6) motion tests the sufficiency of the complaint. To survive, a complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to "state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face." Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007). "Threadbare recitals of the elements of a cause of action, supported by mere conclusory statements, do not suffice." Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009). The Court must "strip" conclusory allegations and determine whether the remaining well-pleaded facts plausibly entitle the plaintiff to relief. Id. at 679.

The Court may consider documents "referred to in the plaintiff\'s complaint and... central to the plaintiff\'s claim" without converting the motion into one for summary judgment. Lormand v. US Unwired, Inc., 565 F.3d 228, 231--32 (5th Cir. 2009). The MSLSA, SOW-1, the four Change Orders, the written proposal (Exhibit H), the sales presentation (Exhibit G), Dr. Okonkwo\'s March 8, 2023 email (Exhibit E), and the Grayson Whitmore damages analysis (Exhibit F) are all referenced in the FAC and central to Arcadia\'s claims, and may be considered. Where the FAC\'s allegations are contradicted by such documents, the documents control. Claims sounding in fraud must also satisfy the particularity requirements of Rule 9(b). Benchmark Elecs., Inc. v. J.M. Huber Corp., 343 F.3d 719, 724 (5th Cir. 2003).

Choice of law. The MSLSA\'s Section 12.7 selects Delaware law to govern the contract; the FAC\'s assertion that "Texas law governs this breach of contract claim" (FAC ¶ 76) is therefore incorrect. The tort claims likely implicate Texas law under the most-significant-relationship test, but they fail under either Delaware or Texas law, as shown below. The DTPA is a Texas statute. Unjust enrichment is governed by forum law.

**IV. ARGUMENT**

**A. Count V (DTPA) Should Be Dismissed Because the Transaction Is Statutorily Exempt and the Alleged Misrepresentations Are Non-Actionable Puffery.**

1\. The Section 17.49(f) exemption applies. Section 17.49(f) of the Texas Business & Commerce Code exempts from DTPA coverage any "causes of action arising from a transaction, a project, or a set of transactions relating to the same project, involving total consideration by the consumer of more than \$500,000" where the consumer has assets of \$25 million or more. Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 17.49(f). The total consideration here is \$17,050,000 (\$14.7 million under the MSLSA plus \$2.35 million in Change Orders)---more than thirty-four times the \$500,000 threshold. FAC ¶¶ 34, 46, 100. The \$25 million asset threshold is likewise satisfied: the FAC alleges that Arcadia made "substantial infrastructure investments exceeding \$30 million" in connection with the deployment (FAC ¶¶ 38, 117), and Arcadia\'s own damages expert reports that Arcadia\'s "total assets as of \[its\] most recent audited financial statements are approximately \$41 million." The exemption is dispositive. See PPG Indus., Inc. v. JMB/Houston Ctrs. Partners Ltd., 146 S.W.3d 79, 89 (Tex. App.---Houston \[1st Dist.\] 2004, no pet.) (exemption determined by total consideration at the time of the transaction).

Arcadia\'s own submissions are internally inconsistent on the asset question: Arcadia\'s counsel represented in a July 25, 2023 letter that Arcadia\'s total assets were "\$23.8 million," while Arcadia\'s damages expert reported "approximately \$41 million" three days later. That contradiction---together with the FAC\'s own allegation of \$30 million in infrastructure investments---confirms that the asset threshold is met or, at minimum, that the exemption cannot be resolved against Meridian on the face of the complaint. The burden of establishing an exemption rests on the party asserting it, Cameron v. Terrell & Garrett, Inc., 618 S.W.2d 535, 541 (Tex. 1981), and Meridian has met that burden through Arcadia\'s own admissions.

2\. Even if the exemption were not dispositive, the DTPA claim fails on the merits. The alleged misrepresentations are non-actionable puffery. "Industry-leading performance for healthcare analytics" and "30-40% improvement in reporting efficiency" are vague, subjective claims of product superiority and generalized projections upon which no reasonable sophisticated buyer would rely. Pizza Hut, Inc. v. Papa John\'s Int\'l, Inc., 227 F.3d 489, 497 (5th Cir. 2000); Castrol Inc. v. Pennzoil Co., 987 F.2d 939, 945 (3d Cir. 1993). The written proposal expressly disclaimed these metrics as "provided for planning purposes only" and "not... guarantees." And the FAC\'s centerpiece---that Poletti said NexusCore "will integrate seamlessly with any EHR platform" (FAC ¶ 25)---is directly contradicted by the written proposal, which states that "Meridian does not warrant that integration with any particular EHR system will be seamless, error-free, or achievable within any specific timeframe," and by the sales presentation, which qualified integration as "subject to proper configuration."

3\. Arcadia also cannot establish producing cause. The DTPA requires that the deceptive act be a "producing cause" of the plaintiff\'s damages. Melody Home Mfg. Co. v. Barnes, 741 S.W.2d 349, 351--52 (Tex. 1987). The implementation delays and post-Go-Live data issues on which Arcadia\'s damages rest were caused by Arcadia\'s own breaches (the 45-day Project Manager gap and 75-day-late API specifications) and by Linden Park\'s data-migration errors---not by any pre-sale statement. That intervening conduct breaks the causal chain to any alleged deceptive act.

**B. Count IV (Unjust Enrichment) Should Be Dismissed Because an Express Contract Governs the Subject Matter.**

Unjust enrichment is an equitable remedy unavailable where a valid, express contract covers the subject matter of the dispute. Fortune Prod. Co. v. Conoco, Inc., 52 S.W.3d 671, 684 (Tex. 2000); Excess Underwriters at Lloyd\'s v. Frank\'s Casing Crew & Rental Tools, Inc., 246 S.W.3d 42, 59--60 (Tex. 2008). Arcadia does not allege that the MSLSA is void or unenforceable; to the contrary, Count I is premised on the existence and breach of a "valid and enforceable written contract." FAC ¶ 69. The MSLSA indisputably governs the entire subject matter of the parties\' software-licensing and implementation relationship. Counts I and IV are therefore irreconcilably inconsistent.

Arcadia\'s likely response---that the limitation of liability and consequential-damages waiver prevent full recovery under the contract---is squarely foreclosed. The Texas Supreme Court has held that a plaintiff\'s inability to recover under the contract "does not create a 'gap' that unjust enrichment can fill." Excess Underwriters, 246 S.W.3d at 59--60. "Equity follows the law": where parties have allocated risk through contract, equity will not rewrite the bargain. Count IV should be dismissed.

**C. Count II (Fraud) Should Be Dismissed for Four Independent Reasons.**

1\. The FAC fails Rule 9(b). Rule 9(b) requires a plaintiff to specify the "who, what, when, where, and why" of the alleged fraud. Benchmark Elecs., 343 F.3d at 724. The FAC identifies Poletti and the approximate dates of the presentations, but it does not plead facts showing why the statements were false when made. Arcadia\'s theory is entirely retrospective: because the implementation was delayed and performance issues emerged post-Go-Live, Meridian "must have known" its pre-sale statements were false. FAC ¶¶ 30, 56--60. Post-hoc product failure, without more, does not satisfy the "why" prong. Dorsey v. Portfolio Equities, Inc., 540 F.3d 333, 339 (5th Cir. 2008). The FAC\'s "on information and belief" allegations of concealment (FAC ¶¶ 56--60) are unaccompanied by any stated factual basis, which Rule 9(b) forbids. Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Income Fund, Inc. v. TXU Corp., 565 F.3d 200, 207--08 (5th Cir. 2009).

2\. The economic loss doctrine bars the fraud claim. All of Arcadia\'s damages are purely economic, they relate to the subject matter of the MSLSA (the quality and performance of NexusCore), and Meridian owes no duty independent of the contract. Under Delaware law, that combination bars tort recovery. Brasby v. Morris Dynamics, Inc., 947 A.2d 1042, 1049--50 (Del. 2008); Kuhn Constr., Inc. v. Diamond State Port Corp., 990 A.2d 393, 401--02 (Del. 2010). The same result obtains under Texas law, which applies a duty-focused analysis: where the alleged "fraud" is merely a repackaging of a breach-of-contract claim---"the defendant promised X and did not deliver X"---the economic loss rule bars the tort claim. Sharyland Water Supply Corp. v. City of Alton, 354 S.W.3d 407, 415--16 (Tex. 2011); Chapman Custom Homes, Inc. v. Dallas Plumbing Co., 445 S.W.3d 716, 718--19 (Tex. 2014). Arcadia\'s fraud claim is precisely such a repackaging.

3\. The alleged statements are non-actionable puffery, and reliance was not justifiable. "Industry-leading performance" and "30-40% improvement" are puffery. Pizza Hut, 227 F.3d at 497; Presidio Enters., Inc. v. Warner Bros. Distributing Corp., 784 F.2d 674, 679--80 (5th Cir. 1986) (sophisticated commercial buyer cannot reasonably rely on a seller\'s general promotional statements, particularly after conducting its own due diligence). Arcadia---a \$62-million company advised by counsel---conducted reference calls and a proof-of-concept trial, and its own CTO acknowledged in writing that integration would be complex and require a skilled integrator. Justifiable reliance cannot be established on those facts. Hollcroft-Gravitt & Sedgwick, L.L.P. v. Pac. Mut. Life Ins. Co., 51 S.W.3d 573, 577 (Tex. 2001).

4\. The integration clause bars reliance on extra-contractual representations. Section 12.1 supersedes "all prior and contemporaneous agreements, proposals, representations, and understandings, whether oral or written," and each party acknowledged that it "has not relied upon any statement, representation, warranty, or agreement of the other Party except as expressly set forth in this Agreement." Under Delaware law, a party to a fully negotiated agreement with such an integration clause "cannot... assert a claim for fraud based on those very representations." Eagle Indus., Inc. v. DeVilbiss Health Care, Inc., 702 A.2d 1228, 1232 (Del. 1997); see also SIGA Techs., Inc. v. PharmAthene, Inc., 67 A.3d 330, 344 (Del. 2013). Arcadia expressly withdrew its request for a fraud carve-out to the integration clause during negotiations, confirming that it elected to proceed on the contract\'s terms alone.

**D. Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation) Should Be Dismissed Because There Is No Independent Duty and the Damages Are Contractual.**

Negligent misrepresentation under Texas law requires a duty of care independent of any contractual obligation. McCamish, Martin, Brown & Loeffler v. F.E. Appling Interests, 991 S.W.2d 787, 792 (Tex. 1999). Meridian\'s relationship with Arcadia is purely contractual; there is no fiduciary, professional, or statutory duty supplying information beyond the MSLSA\'s express warranty and disclaimer provisions. The claim therefore fails for lack of an independent duty. See also Sharyland, 354 S.W.3d at 415--16; Chapman, 445 S.W.3d at 718--19.

The claim also fails because Arcadia\'s damages are "benefit-of-the-bargain" losses---the difference between what Arcadia says it was promised and what it received---which are recoverable only in contract. Fed. Land Bank Ass\'n of Tyler v. Sloane, 825 S.W.2d 439, 442--43 (Tex. 1992). Arcadia seeks the contract value, lost profits, and increased operating costs---all contractual in nature---not out-of-pocket reliance losses. Finally, because the negligent-mispresentation claim sounds in fraud, it must satisfy Rule 9(b), which it does not for the reasons stated in Section IV.C.1. Benchmark Elecs., 343 F.3d at 724 (Rule 9(b) applies to claims "sounding in fraud").

**E. Count I (Breach of Contract) Should Be Dismissed, or in the Alternative, Arcadia\'s Damages Should Be Limited to the Contractual Cap.**

1\. Deemed acceptance. Section 5.3 gave Arcadia 30 days following Go-Live (January 15, 2023) to deliver written notice of material nonconformity; the window closed on February 14, 2023. Arcadia did not deliver any written notice until Dr. Okonkwo\'s March 8, 2023 email---22 days late---which referenced only "intermittent latency issues" and "occasional report generation errors." FAC ¶ 50. Under the express terms of Section 5.3, acceptance was deemed granted. Deemed-acceptance provisions in software contracts are enforceable bright-line rules, and oral or post-deadline complaints do not satisfy the contractual written-notice requirement. Simulados, Inc. v. Canton Health Mgmt. Co., 2019 WL 4573218, at \*5--6 (W.D. Tex. Sept. 20, 2019); Precision Healthcare Sols. v. Nextera Data Sys., 458 F. Supp. 3d 544, 551--52 (N.D. Tex. 2020) (deemed acceptance applies to latent as well as patent defects). Once acceptance was deemed granted, Arcadia\'s remedies for alleged defects were limited to the warranty and maintenance provisions, not pre-acceptance rejection.

2\. The limited warranty has expired, and the exclusive remedy controls. Section 9.1\'s limited warranty ran for 90 days from Go-Live and expired on April 15, 2023. The sole remedy for breach of that warranty is repair, replacement, or, if Meridian could not cure within 60 days, a pro-rata refund of pre-paid license fees. Section 8.3 designates the contract\'s remedies as exclusive. Arcadia\'s \$47.3 million demand bears no relationship to these exclusive remedies.

3\. The data-migration defects were Arcadia\'s responsibility. The FAC\'s breach allegations rest heavily on "data integrity failures" and "data corruption" (FAC ¶¶ 43, 52, 72(d)). But SOW-1 Section 3.2 and Exhibit C expressly allocated sole responsibility for data migration to Arcadia, and Arcadia retained Linden Park to perform it. CO-004---executed by Arcadia---expressly acknowledges that the data-integrity issues "arose from errors in data migration scripts prepared and executed by Linden Park... and not from any defect in the NexusCore software or any act or omission of \[Meridian\]." Twelve of the 47 support tickets were traceable to Linden Park\'s migration. Arcadia cannot convert its own (and its contractor\'s) data-migration failures into a Meridian breach.

4\. The 137-day delay was caused by Arcadia and Linden Park. The August 25, 2022 project status report attributes the delay to Arcadia\'s 45-day Project Manager gap, its 75-day-late API specifications, and Linden Park\'s migration-script failures---all client-side or third-party dependencies outside Meridian\'s control. Section 3.5 of the MSLSA provides for day-for-day schedule adjustments caused by Arcadia\'s failure to fulfill its obligations. The FAC\'s conclusory assertion that Meridian caused the delay (FAC ¶¶ 40--46) is contradicted by the contemporaneous project record.

5\. Alternative: damages are contractually capped and consequential damages are waived. Should the Court decline to dismiss Count I in full, Arcadia\'s damages must be limited as a matter of law. Section 8.2 categorically excludes the \$18.4 million in lost profits, the \$6.7 million in increased operating costs, and the \$5 million in reputational harm---all consequential or intangible losses. Section 8.1 caps aggregate liability at the fees paid or payable in the twelve months preceding the event. Limitation-of-liability and consequential-damages-waiver provisions in negotiated commercial software agreements between sophisticated parties are enforceable, and a consequential-damages waiver survives independently even if a limited remedy fails of its essential purpose. Dresser-Rand Co. v. Virtual Automation, Inc., 361 F.3d 831, 838--40 (5th Cir. 2004); Kana Software, Inc. v. Sealand Tech., Inc., 178 A.3d 1045, 1058--60 (Del. Ch. 2017). Arcadia\'s \$47.3 million demand is therefore barred in substantial part by the very bargain Arcadia\'s counsel negotiated and Arcadia\'s leadership accepted.

**V. CONCLUSION**

For the foregoing reasons, Meridian respectfully requests that the Court grant this motion and dismiss all five counts of the First Amended Complaint with prejudice, and in the alternative, dismiss Counts II, III, IV, and V with prejudice and limit the damages recoverable on Count I to the contractual cap and the exclusive remedies set forth in the MSLSA, together with such other and further relief as the Court deems just and proper.

Dated: January 15, 2024

Respectfully submitted,

**STONEBRIDGE & CALLOWAY LLP**

/s/ Margaret "Meg" Calloway

Margaret "Meg" Calloway

Texas State Bar No. 24037891

David Arsenault

Texas State Bar No. 24098254

600 Congress Avenue, Suite 2800

Austin, Texas 78701

Telephone: (512) 555-4200

Facsimile: (512) 555-4201

mcalloway@stonebridgecalloway.com

darsenault@stonebridgecalloway.com

*Attorneys for Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.*

**CERTIFICATE OF CONFERENCE**

Pursuant to the Court\'s Standing Order § 3.1, counsel for Meridian conferred in good faith with counsel for Arcadia (Jonathan Breckenridge, Esq., Wexford Hale LLP) by telephone on January 8, 2024, regarding the relief sought in this motion. Arcadia\'s counsel indicated that Arcadia opposes dismissal of any count and declined to voluntarily narrow or dismiss any claim. The parties were unable to reach agreement. For this reason, and because the motion raises legal questions on which no compromise is practicable, Meridian submits this motion.

**CERTIFICATE OF SERVICE**

I hereby certify that on January 15, 2024, I electronically filed the foregoing Motion to Dismiss with the Clerk of Court using the CM/ECF system, which will send notification of such filing to all counsel of record, including Jonathan Breckenridge, Wexford Hale LLP, 300 West 6th Street, Suite 1500, Austin, Texas 78701, jbreckenridge@wexfordhale.com, counsel for Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC.

**ORDER GRANTING DEFENDANT\'S MOTION TO DISMISS**

**PURSUANT TO FEDERAL RULE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE 12(b)(6)**

On this day came on to be considered Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.\'s Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff\'s First Amended Complaint Pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(6) (Dkt. No. \_\_) (the "Motion"), together with Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC\'s response thereto and Defendant\'s reply, if any. The Court, having reviewed the Motion, the parties\' briefing, the First Amended Complaint (Dkt. No. 12), the Master Software License and Services Agreement and Statement of Work #1 central to Plaintiff\'s claims, and the applicable law, is of the opinion that the Motion should be granted.

It is therefore ORDERED, ADJUDGED, and DECREED that the Motion is GRANTED.

It is further ORDERED that all five counts of Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC\'s First Amended Complaint---Count I (Breach of Contract), Count II (Fraud), Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation), Count IV (Unjust Enrichment), and Count V (Violation of the Texas Deceptive Trade Practices--Consumer Protection Act)---are DISMISSED WITH PREJUDICE.

It is further ORDERED that, in the alternative and only in the event the Court\'s dismissal of Count I is reversed or modified on appeal, Arcadia\'s recoverable damages on Count I shall be limited to the exclusive remedies and the limitation of liability set forth in the Master Software License and Services Agreement, including the aggregate-liability cap in Section 8.1 and the consequential-damages waiver in Section 8.2, and that all claims for lost profits, increased operating costs, reputational harm, punitive damages, treble damages, and attorneys\' fees are barred.

It is further ORDERED that Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC shall take nothing by its First Amended Complaint, that all claims against Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. are dismissed with prejudice, and that Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. is entitled to its reasonable and necessary attorneys\' fees and costs to the extent permitted by applicable law and the terms of the Master Software License and Services Agreement.

It is further ORDERED that this Order is final and appealable as to all claims and all parties, and that the Clerk is directed to enter judgment accordingly.

**IT IS SO ORDERED.**

SIGNED this \_\_\_\_\_ day of \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_, 2024.

**1
result:
**STONEBRIDGE & CALLOWAY LLP**

600 Congress Avenue, Suite 2800 • Austin, Texas 78701

**PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL --- ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT**

**MEMORANDUM**

**TO:** Margaret "Meg" Calloway, Lead Partner; David Arsenault, Senior Associate

**FROM:** Litigation Team, Stonebridge & Calloway LLP

**DATE:** January 12, 2024

**RE:** Threshold and Strategic Issues --- Defendant\'s Rule 12(b)(6) Motion to Dismiss --- Arcadia Health Systems, LLC v. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc., No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA (W.D. Tex., Austin Div.)

This memorandum flags the threshold and strategic issues the team should resolve before and shortly after filing the attached motion to dismiss, proposed order, and this cover memo. The issues are listed in approximate order of urgency. The motion itself is drafted to move to dismiss all five counts; the points below concern matters that either sit outside the four corners of the brief, that may affect how or whether the brief is filed, or that require follow-up.

**1. Subject-Matter Jurisdiction --- A Likely Complete-Diversity Defect (HIGHEST PRIORITY)**

Our own Notice of Removal alleged that Arcadia is a citizen of Texas only, based solely on Dr. Okonkwo\'s domicile. That analysis is almost certainly wrong and may be fatal to the Court\'s subject-matter jurisdiction. An LLC\'s citizenship is determined by the citizenship of all of its members. Harvey v. Grey Wolf Drilling Co., 542 F.3d 1077, 1080 (5th Cir. 2008). Arcadia\'s operating agreement (produced as Exhibit 7) and the FAC itself (¶¶ 7--8) disclose three members: Dr. Okonkwo (Texas), Martin Schreiber (Texas), and Apex Medical Ventures, LP (a Delaware limited partnership). A limited partnership\'s citizenship is traced to all of its partners. Carden v. Arkoma Assocs., 494 U.S. 185, 195--96 (1990). Apex\'s general partner is Apex Medical Ventures GP, Inc., a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business in Wilmington, Delaware---a citizen of Delaware. Apex\'s limited partner is the Okonkwo Family Trust (beneficiary domiciled in Texas).

Accordingly, Arcadia is a citizen of both Texas and Delaware (through Apex GP, Inc.). Meridian is a citizen of both Delaware (incorporation) and Texas (principal place of business, Austin). Because both parties share Delaware and Texas citizenship, complete diversity does not exist, and the Court likely lacks subject-matter jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1332(a). Subject-matter jurisdiction cannot be waived and may be raised by the Court sua sponte at any time. Arbaugh v. Y & H Corp., 546 U.S. 500, 514 (2006).

Strategic implications. This is awkward because Meridian removed the case and would be moving to remand on a defect it created. Options to discuss with the client: (a) file the 12(b)(6) motion on the merits and let the jurisdictional issue be raised sua sponte or by Arcadia (risk: the Court may remand and the motion becomes moot, but the substantive defenses travel with the case to state court); (b) move to remand ourselves (cleanest legally, but concedes our removal was improper and forfeits the federal forum and Judge Alvarez\'s rigorous pleading standards); (c) file the 12(b)(6) motion and concurrently flag the jurisdictional issue to the Court. We should confirm the citizenship analysis against the Apex partnership agreement (still outstanding) and decide before the January 15 deadline. Note: the FAC\'s own allegations (¶¶ 7--8) disclose the membership structure that destroys diversity, so the defect is apparent on the face of the operative pleading.

**2. Post-Answer Motion --- Rule 12(b)(6) vs. Rule 12(c)**

There is a direct conflict between the Court\'s Standing Order and the Scheduling Order. Standing Order § 3.4 states that a post-answer motion preserved in the Answer "shall \[be\] treat\[ed\]... as a motion to dismiss, not as a motion for judgment on the pleadings under Rule 12(c)." The Scheduling Order states the opposite: "The Court will treat any such motion as a Rule 12(c) motion for judgment on the pleadings, evaluated under the same standard as a Rule 12(b)(6) motion." The Answer preserved the defense (First Affirmative Defense; Part III reservation; letter to the Court dated November 8, 2023), so preservation is not in dispute. The practical effect is minimal because the standard is identical (Twombly/Iqbal), and on a 12(c) motion the Court may also consider the Answer. The motion is captioned as a 12(b)(6) motion per the task and the Standing Order; we should be prepared for the Court to treat it as 12(c) and should not object.

**3. Choice of Law --- The FAC Misstates the Governing Law for Count I**

Section 12.7 of the MSLSA selects Delaware law, without regard to conflict-of-law principles, to govern "this Agreement." The FAC nevertheless pleads that "Texas law governs this breach of contract claim" (FAC ¶ 76) and "this fraud claim" (¶ 89), "this negligent misrepresentation claim" (¶ 97), and "this unjust enrichment claim" (¶ 104). The motion argues Delaware law governs Count I and that the tort claims fail under either Delaware or Texas law. We should be prepared to brief choice of law more fully if Arcadia contests it. The DTPA (Count V) is a Texas statute regardless of the choice-of-law clause. For unjust enrichment (Count IV), both Delaware and Texas bar the claim where an express contract governs, so the choice of law is immaterial to the result.

**4. DTPA --- The Section 17.49(f) Asset Threshold and a Contradiction in Arcadia\'s Own Submissions**

The § 17.49(f) exemption is our cleanest DTPA ground, but it has a wrinkle. Arcadia\'s own July 25, 2023 letter confirms that the current statute is conjunctive: it requires both (a) total consideration exceeding \$500,000 and (b) the consumer having assets of \$25 million or more. The \$500,000 element is indisputably met (\$17.05 million). The \$25 million asset element is where the record is messy:

• Arcadia\'s counsel represented in the July 25, 2023 letter that Arcadia\'s total assets were "\$23.8 million"---below the threshold.

• Arcadia\'s own damages expert (Grayson Whitmore, July 28, 2023) reported Arcadia\'s total assets at "approximately \$41 million"---above the threshold.

• The FAC alleges "substantial infrastructure investments exceeding \$30 million" (FAC ¶¶ 38, 117), which supports (though does not conclusively establish) assets above \$25 million.

This direct contradiction in Arcadia\'s own submissions is both a litigation asset and a complication. On a 12(b)(6) motion, the Court may be reluctant to find the exemption established if Arcadia\'s asset size is not conclusively shown on the face of the complaint. The motion therefore argues the exemption is satisfied (relying on the FAC\'s \$30 million investment allegation and the \$41 million figure in Arcadia\'s own damages report) and, in the alternative, presses the puffery, disclaimer, and producing-cause grounds so that the DTPA count fails even if the asset threshold is not resolved at the pleading stage. We should obtain Arcadia\'s audited financial statements (counsel for Arcadia offered to produce them) and be prepared to renew the § 17.49(f) argument on summary judgment if needed. The burden of proving the exemption is on Meridian. Cameron v. Terrell & Garrett, Inc., 618 S.W.2d 535, 541 (Tex. 1981).

**5. DTPA --- Verify the Current Text of Section 17.49(f)**

The record contains inconsistent statements of the statute. Our research memo states the exemption as "\$500,000 + consumer not an individual." Arcadia\'s letter, our Answer (Thirteenth Affirmative Defense), and our pre-suit response letter state it as "\$500,000 + \$25 million in assets." These are not the same test. We must verify the current statutory text (and any 2011/2019 amendments) before the brief is finalized and align the motion, the Answer, and our pre-suit correspondence. The motion currently tracks the conjunctive \$500,000 + \$25 million-assets formulation as stated by Arcadia\'s own counsel.

**6. DTPA --- Possible Failure to Satisfy the 60-Day Pre-Suit Notice (Section 17.505(a))**

Section 17.505(a) requires a consumer to give written notice at least 60 days before filing suit. Arcadia\'s DTPA demand letter is dated June 15, 2023; the original petition was filed August 7, 2023---only 53 days later. (There is also a date discrepancy: our July 10, 2023 response refers to Arcadia\'s letter as dated "June 26, 2023," which would make the gap only 42 days.) Either way, suit appears to have been filed before the 60-day period elapsed. This is a procedural defect more naturally raised by a motion to abate or in the answer than by a 12(b)(6) motion, and courts often treat it as curable rather than jurisdictional. We have not made it a primary ground in the motion, but it should be raised separately (or held in reserve) and the demand-letter date reconciled.

**7. Jury-Trial Waiver (Section 12.9)**

The MSLSA contains a mutual jury-trial waiver, which Arcadia\'s counsel expressly accepted during negotiations. The Scheduling Order recites that "\[t\]he parties have waived their right to a jury trial pursuant to Section 12.9" and sets a bench trial. The FAC nonetheless demands a jury (FAC ¶ 122), and our Answer conditionally demands a jury "to the extent that the Court determines the jury trial waiver... is not enforceable." Watch for an Arcadia challenge to the waiver on the theory that fraudulent inducement voids the waiver or that the waiver does not extend to the fraud/DTPA claims. This is a trial-phase issue, not a motion-to-dismiss issue, but it should be tracked.

**8. Documents the Court May Consider on the Motion**

The motion relies on documents beyond the FAC. Under Lormand, the Court may consider documents referenced in and central to the claims without converting the motion to summary judgment. Safest: the MSLSA, SOW-1, and the four Change Orders (all referenced in the FAC and central to every count). Also strong: the written proposal (Exhibit H), the sales presentation (Exhibit G), Dr. Okonkwo\'s March 8, 2023 email (Exhibit E), and the Grayson Whitmore damages analysis (Exhibit F)---all listed in the FAC\'s exhibit list. Pushing the boundary: the Schreiber internal memo and the August 25, 2022 project status report, which are referenced in the FAC (¶¶ 31, 44) and central to reliance and causation but were not attached. Be prepared for Arcadia to argue that consideration of the project-status report and Schreiber memo converts the motion to summary judgment; we should be ready to respond that both are referenced in and central to the claims, or to rely primarily on the contract documents and the FAC\'s own allegations.

**9. Procedural Compliance with the Standing Order**

• Certificate of conference. Standing Order § 3.1 requires a certificate of conference for dispositive motions, filed as a separate document. The motion includes a certificate of conference; we should also file it as a standalone document to be safe, and ensure the meet-and-confer with Breckenridge actually occurs and is documented before filing.

• Page limit. The motion is within the 25-page limit (Standing Order § 3.2), exclusive of the caption, signature block, and certificates.

• Reply page limit conflict. The Standing Order allows 15-page replies; the Scheduling Order says 10-page replies. We should plan for 10 pages to be safe.

• Proposed order. Standing Order § 3.5 requires the proposed order as a separate .docx filing (not embedded in the motion). The attached proposed order is drafted accordingly; update the docket entry number within one business day of filing.

• Deadline. The dispositive-motion deadline is January 15, 2024 (Scheduling Order § III.A).

**10. Strategy on Count I --- Full Dismissal vs. Damages Limitation**

The motion seeks full dismissal of Count I but, in the alternative, asks that damages be limited to the contractual cap and exclusive remedies. Full dismissal of a breach-of-contract claim at the pleading stage is aggressive, and Judge Alvarez may be reluctant to dismiss it entirely where the FAC alleges non-delivery of conforming software. The deemed-acceptance and warranty-expiration arguments are strong, but the safer play may be to press the damages-limitation alternative heavily. The proposed order grants full dismissal but, in the alternative, limits damages---matching the motion\'s primary/alternative structure. We should be prepared for the Court to deny full dismissal of Count I but grant the damages limitation, which would still eliminate roughly \$30 million of the \$47.3 million demand.

**11. The "Seamless Integration" Representation --- Evidentiary Note**

The FAC alleges Poletti said NexusCore "will integrate seamlessly with any EHR platform" (FAC ¶ 25). Our Answer avers that Poletti\'s contemporaneous notes reflect that he actually said integration is "subject to proper configuration." The contemporaneous notes are extrinsic and should not be the primary basis at the pleading stage. Instead, the motion relies on the written proposal (Exhibit H), which expressly disclaims "seamless" integration, and the sales presentation (Exhibit G), which qualifies integration as "subject to proper configuration." Both are referenced in the FAC and central to the claims. Preserve the Poletti notes for summary judgment or trial.

**12. Outstanding Factual Items**

• Confirm the citizenship analysis with the Apex Medical Ventures, LP partnership agreement (still outstanding).

• Obtain Arcadia\'s audited financial statements to nail down the § 17.49(f) asset threshold (Arcadia\'s counsel offered to produce them).

• Reconcile the DTPA demand-letter date (June 15 vs. June 26, 2023) for the § 17.505(a) 60-day-notice analysis.

• Obtain Linden Park\'s scope-of-work and correspondence to strengthen the causation/data-migration arguments.

• Calculate the Section 8.1 cap amount (fees paid or payable in the 12 months preceding the "event giving rise to liability") from Meridian\'s billing records; the "event" date is ambiguous and affects the cap.

Please advise on the jurisdictional question (Item 1) at your earliest convenience, as it may affect whether and how we file on January 15.

{"file_path": "response.md", "content": "# Deliverables — Arcadia Health Systems, LLC v. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc., No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA (W.D. Tex.)

Three documents were produced in `output/`, each formatted to Judge Alvarez's Standing Order (Times New Roman 12pt, double-spaced, 1-inch margins, bottom-center page numbering, proper federal caption) and validated as schema-correct .docx files.

## 1. `motion-to-dismiss.docx` (19 pages; within the 25-page limit)

A Rule 12(b)(6) motion to dismiss all five counts of the First Amended Complaint, filed as a preserved post-answer motion (Answer, Dkt. No. 14, First Affirmative Defense + Part III reservation). It moves to dismiss each count on independent grounds drawn from the record:

- **Count V (DTPA)** — Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 17.49(f) exemption (total consideration $17.05M ≫ $500,000; $25M asset threshold supported by the FAC's $30M-investment allegation and Arcadia's own $41M damages-report figure); alternatively, non-actionable puffery (*Pizza Hut*, *Castrol*, *Presidio*), the written proposal's express disclaimer of "seamless" integration, and no producing cause (*Melody Home*).
- **Count IV (Unjust Enrichment)** — barred by the express MSLSA covering the same subject matter (*Fortune Production*; *Excess Underwriters*); the LoL/consequential-damages waiver creates no "gap" equity may fill.
- **Count II (Fraud)** — four independent grounds: Rule 9(b) failure (*Benchmark*, *Dorsey*, *Flaherty*); economic loss doctrine under both Delaware (*Brasby*, *Kuhn*) and Texas (*Sharyland*, *Chapman*); puffery / no justifiable reliance (*Pizza Hut*, *Presidio*, *Hollcroft*); and the integration clause / anti-reliance doctrine (*Eagle Industries*, *SIGA*), reinforced by Arcadia's withdrawal of its fraud carve-out.
- **Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation)** — no independent duty (*McCamish*); benefit-of-the-bargain damages recoverable only in contract (*Sloane*); economic loss rule; Rule 9(b) applies to claims sounding in fraud.
- **Count I (Breach of Contract)** — deemed acceptance under § 5.3 (first written complaint 22 days after the Feb. 14, 2023 window closed; *Simulados*, *Precision Healthcare*); expired 90-day warranty and exclusive remedies (§§ 9.1, 8.3); data-migration responsibility allocated to Arcadia (SOW-1 § 3.2; CO-004's acknowledgment that Linden Park caused the errors); delay caused by Arcadia (45-day PM gap, 75-day-late API specs) and Linden Park. **Alternative:** damages capped by § 8.1 and consequential damages waived by § 8.2 (*Dresser-Rand*, *Kana Software*), eliminating ~$30M of the $47.3M demand.

Includes a certificate of conference and certificate of service per the Standing Order.

## 2. `proposed-order.docx`

A separate, self-executing proposed order (per Standing Order § 3.5) granting the motion, dismissing all five counts with prejudice, with an alternative damages-limitation paragraph keyed to Count I and a signature line for Judge Alvarez.

## 3. `cover-memo.docx` (privileged / work product)

An internal memo flagging threshold and strategic issues, in priority order:

1. **Subject-matter jurisdiction (highest priority)** — a likely complete-diversity defect: Arcadia (via Apex Medical Ventures GP, Inc., a Delaware-citizen general partner) is a Delaware citizen, as is Meridian; our own Notice of Removal incorrectly treated Arcadia as Texas-only. SMJ is non-waivable and may be raised sua sponte (*Arbaugh*); strategic options discussed.
2. Post-answer motion — 12(b)(6) vs. 12(c) conflict between the Standing Order and the Scheduling Order (same standard; preservation not in dispute).
3. Choice of law — MSLSA § 12.7 selects Delaware law; the FAC misstates Texas law for Count I.
4. DTPA § 17.49(f) — the $25M asset threshold and a direct contradiction in Arcadia's own submissions ($23.8M per counsel's July 25, 2023 letter vs. ~$41M per the July 28, 2023 damages report).
5. Verify the current text of § 17.49(f) (the record contains inconsistent formulations).
6. DTPA § 17.505(a) 60-day pre-suit notice — suit filed 53 days after the June 15, 2023 demand (date discrepancy noted).

{"file_path": "response.md", "content": "# Deliverables — Arcadia Health Systems, LLC v. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc., No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA (W.D. Tex.)

Three documents were produced in `output/`, each formatted to Judge Alvarez's Standing Order (Times New Roman 12pt, double-spaced, 1-inch margins, bottom-center page numbering, proper federal caption) and validated as schema-correct .docx files.

## 1. `motion-to-dismiss.docx` (19 pages; within the 25-page limit)

A Rule 12(b)(6) motion to dismiss all five counts of the First Amended Complaint, filed as a preserved post-answer motion (Answer, Dkt. No. 14, First Affirmative Defense + Part III reservation). It moves to dismiss each count on independent grounds drawn from the record:

- **Count V (DTPA)** — Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 17.49(f) exemption (total consideration $17.05M ≫ $500,000; $25M asset threshold supported by the FAC's $30M-investment allegation and Arcadia's own $41M damages-report figure); alternatively, non-actionable puffery (*Pizza Hut*, *Castrol*, *Presidio*), the written proposal's express disclaimer of "seamless" integration, and no producing cause (*Melody Home*).
- **Count IV (Unjust Enrichment)** — barred by the express MSLSA covering the same subject matter (*Fortune Production*; *Excess Underwriters*); the LoL/consequential-damages waiver creates no "gap" equity may fill.
- **Count II (Fraud)** — four independent grounds: Rule 9(b) failure (*Benchmark*, *Dorsey*, *Flaherty*); economic loss doctrine under both Delaware (*Brasby*, *Kuhn*) and Texas (*Sharyland*, *Chapman*); puffery / no justifiable reliance (*Pizza Hut*, *Presidio*, *Hollcroft*); and the integration clause / anti-reliance doctrine (*Eagle Industries*, *SIGA*), reinforced by Arcadia's withdrawal of its fraud carve-out.
- **Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation)** — no independent duty (*McCamish*); benefit-of-the-bargain damages recoverable only in contract (*Sloane*); economic loss rule; Rule 9(b) applies to claims sounding in fraud.
- **Count I (Breach of Contract)** — deemed acceptance under § 5.3 (first written complaint 22 days after the Feb. 14, 2023 window closed; *Simulados*, *Precision Healthcare*); expired 90-day warranty and exclusive remedies (§§ 9.1, 8.3); data-migration responsibility allocated to Arcadia (SOW-1 § 3.2; CO-004's acknowledgment that Linden Park caused the errors); delay caused by Arcadia (45-day PM gap, 75-day-late API specs) and Linden Park. **Alternative:** damages capped by § 8.1 and consequential damages waived by § 8.2 (*Dresser-Rand*, *Kana Software*), eliminating ~$30M of the $47.3M demand.

Includes a certificate of conference and certificate of service per the Standing Order.

## 2. `proposed-order.docx`

A separate, self-executing proposed order (per Standing Order § 3.5) granting the motion, dismissing all five counts with prejudice, with an alternative damages-limitation paragraph keyed to Count I and a signature line for Judge Alvarez.

## 3. `cover-memo.docx` (privileged / work product)

An internal memo flagging threshold and strategic issues, in priority order:

1. **Subject-matter jurisdiction (highest priority)** — a likely complete-diversity defect: Arcadia (via Apex Medical Ventures GP, Inc., a Delaware-citizen general partner) is a Delaware citizen, as is Meridian; our own Notice of Removal incorrectly treated Arcadia as Texas-only. SMJ is non-waivable and may be raised sua sponte (*Arbaugh*); strategic options discussed.
2. Post-answer motion — 12(b)(6) vs. 12(c) conflict between the Standing Order and the Scheduling Order (same standard; preservation not in dispute).
3. Choice of law — MSLSA § 12.7 selects Delaware law; the FAC misstates Texas law for Count I.
4. DTPA § 17.49(f) — the $25M asset threshold and a direct contradiction in Arcadia's own submissions ($23.8M per counsel's July 25, 2023 letter vs. ~$41M per the July 28, 2023 damages report).
5. Verify the current text of § 17.49(f) (the record contains inconsistent formulations).
6. DTPA § 17.505(a) 60-day pre-suit notice — suit filed 53 days after the June 15, 2023 demand (date discrepancy noted).
7. Jury-trial waiver (§ 12.9) — watch for a fraudulent-inducement challenge.
8. Which documents the Court may consider on the motion without conversion.
9. Procedural compliance (certificate of conference as separate filing; page limits; reply-limit conflict; proposed-order docket number; Jan. 15, 2024 deadline).
10. Count I strategy — full dismissal vs. damages limitation.
11. Evidentiary note on the "seamless integration" representation (rely on the proposal/presentation, not Poletti's extrinsic notes).
12. Outstanding factual items (Apex partnership agreement; Arcadia audited financials; demand-letter date; Linden Park scope; § 8.1 cap calculation)."}

text:
Confirmed: M-G-6, M-G-7, O-1, O-5, and C-9 are **false negatives** (case-sensitivity / table-cell capture) — those items are actually present. But M-V-8 (DTPA warranty prong) was a **false positive** — my check was too loose; the motion does *not* actually address the § 17.50(a)(2) warranty prong. 

So the **genuinely missing** items are:
1. M-V-7: DTPA "not a consumer" (Riverside Nat'l Bank)
2. M-V-8: DTPA § 17.50(a)(2) breach-of-warranty prong (defeated by § 9.1/§ 9.4)
3. M-V-9: DTPA § 17.50(a)(3) unconscionability prong
4. M-II-3: Rule 9(b) "lumping" (Dorsey)
5. M-III-5: Count III justifiable reliance
6. M-I-3: § 5.3(d) production-use deemed acceptance
7. M-I-15: Count I alternative — speculative/unforeseeable damages
8. C-13: cover memo — post-warranty voluntary patching characterization risk

Let me add all eight with explicit, source-cited treatment. First the motion insertions:
